PREFACE
DOCUMENTS DEPT.

The Colonial Office List 1952 has been compiled from official records and after consultation with Governments. Every effort has been made to ensure the correctness of the information given at the time of publication but the Secretary of State is unable to accept responsibility for the complete accuracy of all details. Readers are invited to draw the Editor's attention to any errors.

The paper shortage has made it necessary to accept a restricted form of publication this year, and for fuller information reference should be made to The Colonial Office List 1951 . The standard plan of headings and sub-headings has been maintained and page references are given throughout to the corresponding sections of the 1951 edition.

Staff lists have been restricted generally, and the Record of Services section has had to be omitted altogether. This last is particularly regretted as the section is used so much for reference on personnel matters; it will be restored in a future edition as soon as circumstances permit. The biggest cut had to be made in Part II, where for the most part amendments only to the 1951 text dealing with the various territories and regional organisations and international co-operation have been included. Part IV is supplementary also, listing only Parliamentary and non-Parliamentary papers of Colonial interest published since the 1951 edition went to press.
A new map of the world, showing the territories dealt with by the Colonial Office, has been introduced in place of the monochrome map used in recent editions.

A revised edition of the companion volume ( The Colonial Office List Map Supplement , published in 1948) had been planned for 1952; but that has had to be deferred. The original edition is still in print and obtainable from Her Majesty's Stationery Office. Copies of the individual maps, which are being revised as necessary by the Directorate of Colonial (Geodetic and Topographic) Surveys, may be ordered from Messrs. Edward Stanford Ltd.
COLONIAL OFFICE, THE CHURCH HOUSE, GREAT SMITH STREET, S.W.1.

PART I THE COLONIAL OFFICE FUNCTIONS AND HISTORY

Constitutional responsibility to Parliament for the good government of British colonial territories rests with the Secretary of State for the Colonies. It is the Minister's responsibility, and the function of his Department, to see that the declared objects of British Colonial policy are carried out, and to marshal and organise the help the Colonies* need.

The actual administration of the Colonies is carried out by the various Colonial Governments, the Governor of each Colony being the representative of H.M. The Queen. Subject to the overriding authority of Parliament, the Colonial Governments enjoy a large and increasing measure of autonomy. Each Colony has its own legislature and its own civil service. These civil services taken together make up the Colonial Service : they are paid from the revenue of the Colonies and are not part of the Home Civil Service.

Most of the Colonies are in tropical areas where the advance of the people has been retarded by unfavourable natural conditions. The Colonial Governments are thus at grips with some of the most complex and, at the same time, most fascinating problems facing the world. They have to bring to bear all the resources of western civilisation in overcoming the natural handicaps which are the lot of so many in the tropics : and as the natural handicaps are overcome, and a sound economy established, they have to guide the people to social betterment and political maturity.

It follows that the work of the Colonial Office touches on every aspect of government. The essence of its work is that it advises, assists and carries out the directions of the Secretary of State on everything pertaining to his responsibility for the good government of Colonial territories. This work involves many and varied activities. Thus the Colonial Office administers the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts, a task which includes the organisation of schemes designed to serve the Colonial Empire as a whole. It recruits for the Colonial Governments the men and women they need for those higher grade administrative and technical posts which cannot be filled locally, and it organises special

training and refresher courses. It is the clearing house for information gained by experience and research throughout the Colonies. It has a staff of Advisers on the principal subjects calling for technical knowledge. Through the co-operation of 300 men and women, eminent in every field of science, public affairs and administration, who sit on its Advisory Committees, it ensures that colonial problems are tackled with the fullest possible background of experience and knowledge. It arranges conferences which facilitate co-operation between the Colonial Powers, and it helps regional development. It is concerned with providing guidance on a multiplicity of problems, covering every aspect of economic and social affairs and ranging from marketing methods to priorities for scarce supplies, from defence to currency, from the organisation of air and other transport services to the use of insecticides. It sends technical advisers into colonial areas to advise on difficulties of organisation and development. It keeps an eye on the welfare of more than 4,500 students from the Colonies who are in Britain, while at the same time encouraging the expansion of education facilities in the Colonies. It is concerned not least with fostering friendship and understanding between the people of Britain and the people of the Colonies and with making colonial affairs widely known in Britain, in the Colonies and abroad.

To cope with this remarkable diversity of tasks, the Colonial Office has an establishment of about 1,200 officers of all grades. The great majority are Home Civil Servants, but it is the practice for a proportion of the administrative and advisory posts to be filled by men with field experience in the Colonies, either seconded for a period or, in a few cases, recently retired from the Colonial Service. At present there are over 50 such men working in senior positions in the Office.
The last few years have seen a notable increase in the number of administrative and advisory posts filled by women.
Details of the organisation of the Office are given in the section which follows under the heading "Distribution of Business". It will be seen that the Office is divided into both geographical and
*The term "Colonies" is used, for convenience, to cover colonies in the strict sense of the term, protectorates, protected states and trust territories.

subject departments, and that there is in addition an Advisory and Research staff. To conduct routine ordering of equipment and supplies, certain classes of recruitment, and various financial functions on behalf of the Colonial Governments, the Secretary of State appoints the Crown Agents for the Colonies, who have a separate office, financed by commission on work done. (see page 15).
History

The necessity to set up an organisation to deal with colonial affairs—which in those days meant the affairs of the settlements in North America and the West Indies—was first recognised in 1660. A Committee of the Privy Council at first undertook the task, but after a few months its responsibilities were taken over in December, 1660, by a "Council of Foreign Plantations". Among those who served on the Council was John Evelyn, whose Diary records his appointment in February, 1671: with the appointment, he adds, went "a salary of £500 to encourage me".

In 1672 the Council was united to the Council for Trade, and was henceforward known as the "Council of Trade and Plantations". It was suppressed in 1677, and its functions, which had been much neglected, were transferred to the Privy Council. It was re-constituted in 1695, and continued to exist until 1782, at which date it consisted of eight Members of Parliament, who received a salary of £1,000 per annum each.

The affairs of India were placed under its charge in 1748, and remained so until the establishment of the Board of Control in 1784. From 1768 Colonial affairs have been dealt with by a Secretary of State.

The office of Secretary to the Sovereign dates at least from the reign of Henry III. There was one principal Secretary only (who was already called Secretary of State) down to 1539, when a second was appointed. From 1708 to 1746 a third Secretaryship existed dealing exclusively with Scotland.

In 1768, a Secretary of State for the American or Colonial Department, was appointed, in addition to the two principal Secretaries of State then existing; but the commissions to the Council of Trade and Plantations continued to run as before. Both the Council and the new Secretary of State's Department were abolished in 1782 by Burke's Act, on the loss of the North American Colonies.
By this Act power was given to delegate to a committee of the Privy Council all the

functions hitherto exercised by the Council of Trade and Plantations, and circular instructions were sent to the Governors of the Plantations to transmit their returns and accounts to the Privy Council. Pending the appointment of a committee, Colonial affairs were dealt with by a subordinate branch of the Home Department, styled the Plantations Branch.

At this time the duties of the two principal Secretaries of State were divided into "Home" and "Foreign," the affairs of Ireland devolving on the Home Department, which now undertook also those of the Colonies.

In 1784 a "Committee for Trade and Foreign Plantations" was appointed in pursuance of Burke's Act, and the new body was reorganised and placed upon a definite footing in 1786. The business hitherto dealt with by the Plantations Branch of the Home Office was transferred to this committee.

At its commencement in 1793, the affairs of the French War were managed by the Home Department, but in 1794 Mr. Dundas (afterwards Lord Melville), who was then the Secretary of State dealing with the Home affairs of the Department, was appointed "Secretary for War," and also nominally Secretary of State for the Colonies; but the Departments of War and the Colonies were not actually united until 1801, when Lord Hobart was created Secretary of State for the War and Colonial Department. From 1794 the "Committee for Trade and Foreign Plantations" (now known as the Board of Trade) gradually ceased to have any connection with Colonial affairs.

From the conclusion of the French War the attention of the Secretary of State was chiefly occupied with the Colonies, and he was usually designated as Secretary of State for the Colonies. In 1854 a fourth principal Secretaryship of State was created—the Secretaryship for War; the affairs of the Colonies then became the entire charge of a principal Secretary of State. In July, 1925, a new Secretaryship of State for Dominion Affairs was set up, to take over from the Colonial Office business connected with the self-governing Dominions.

The office of Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for the Colonies has been in existence since 1830, and a third Ministerial appointment, that of Minister of State for Colonial Affairs, was created in January, 1948, in recognition of the increasingly heavy volume of work at Ministerial level.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE
ADVISORY COMMITTEES
ADVISORY COMMITTEE ON COLONIAL COLLEGES OF ARTS, SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY

This Committee was appointed by the Secretary of State in 1950 to advise him and any responsible authorities in the Colonies on the development of Colonial Colleges of Arts, Science and Technology, and on the expenditure of Colonial Development and Welfare funds allocated for this purpose. The Committee's activities are at present confined to four Colleges : Fourah Bay College (Sierra Leone), the Kumasi College of Technology, the Nigerian College of Arts, Science and Technology, and the Royal Technical College, Nairobi. The Committee will advise on questions referred to it of technical and commercial education at the technical college level arising anywhere in the Colonies.

ADVISORY COMMITTEE ON THE TREATMENT OF OFFENDERS IN THE COLONIES

This Committee was originally established as the Colonial Penal Administration Committee in 1937. In 1943, when the Colonial Social Welfare Advisory Committee was set up, the Colonial Penal Administration Committee was appointed as a sub-committee of that body and was known as the Penal Sub-Committee (later re-named the Treatment of Offenders Sub-Committee).

In January, 1952, it was re-designated the Advisory Committee on the Treatment of Offenders in the Colonies, its terms of reference being : To advise the Secretary of State on such matters relating to the Treatment of Offenders in the Colonial territories as he may decide to refer to it.
The membership of the Committee is :—

Sir Kenneth Roberts-Wray, K.C.M.G. ( Chairman ). F. C. Chambers. W. H. Chinn, C.M.G. Mrs. Violet Creech Jones, J.P. Lord Faringdon. Sir William Fitzgerald, M.C., Q.C. L. W. Fox, C.B., M.C. Miss Margery Fry, J.P. G. H. Heaton, O.B.E. N. R. Hilton. J. E. King, M.B.E. ( Secretary ).

ORDER OF ST. MICHAEL AND ST. GEORGE

The Order of Saint Michael and Saint George is an Order of Chivalry, awards in which are at the disposal of H.M. the Queen. It is available for the recognition ( inter alia ) of services to the Colonial Empire. The Chancery of the Order is at the Colonial Office. The Grand Master is Major-General The Rt. BRITISH COMMONWEALTH FORESTRY CONFERENCE AND THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON BRITISH COMMONWEALTH FORESTRY
25, Savile Row, W.1.
Telephone : REGent 0221.

The Conference meets periodically, usually quinquennially, to discuss questions of forest policy in the Commonwealth and the wider technical aspects of forestry. Owing to the war, there was a gap of twelve years between the meeting in South Africa in 1935 and the meeting in the United Kingdom in 1947 ; the sixth meeting will be held in Canada in 1952. The preparatory work for this Conference and the steps necessary to give effect to the resolutions are carried out by the Standing Committee on British Commonwealth Forestry, composed as follows :—
The Chairman of the British Commonwealth Forestry Conference ; Technical Commissioner of the British Forestry Commission ; Director

of Imperial Forestry Institute ; Member of Council of Empire Forestry Association ; and one representative of each of the following : Colonial Office ; unit of Commonwealth in which last Conference was held ; unit of Commonwealth in which next Conference will be held ; and three co-opted members.
G. D. Rouse ( Secretary ).
BRITISH COUNCIL
65, Davies Street, London, W.1.
Telephone : GROsvenor 8011.
Telegrams : Britcoun Telex London.
See page 38 of The Colonial Office List 1951 .
The Council no longer maintains a representative in Aden.

In 1951/52 provision for British Council work on behalf of Colonial territories amounted to £523,100 of which £80,100 was allocated from Colonial Development and Welfare funds for Colonial student welfare work in the United Kingdom.

During 1950/51 the Council sent from the United Kingdom 11 lecturers and 11 literary and art exhibitions on tours of various Colonies and awarded 31 post-graduate scholarships and 39 bursaries for study in the United Kingdom. Its work within the United Kingdom relating to the Colonies included arranging short courses or individual study programmes for 206 people including 26 United Nations Fellows, meeting 1,950 students on arrival, providing or arranging accommodation for 1,375 and organising a wide range of welfare, instruction and social facilities for students.

COURSES
Medical Statistics and Statistical Methods
The information under the sub-heading Medical Statistics on page 49 of The Colonial Office List 1951 should be replaced by the following :—

A course in statistical methods and their application in medicine and a course in medical statistics and epidemiology will commence in March, 1952, and last for approximately four months. On each course lectures and practical demonstrations are given twice weekly but the courses are so arranged on different days of the week that they can be taken in conjunction. Persons without a medical qualification can be accepted. Fee for each course, £10 10s. 0d. Fee for combined courses, £17 17s. 0d.
Tropical Medicine and Hygiene
The fee for the preliminary (Certificate) part only of the Academic Postgraduate Certificate and Diploma in Tropical Medicine and Hygiene of the University is now £40.

As from the 1st April, 1951, responsibility for the Corporation was transferred from the Minister of Food to the Secretary of State under the provisions of the Overseas Resources Development Act, 1951. By the same Act the functions of the Corporation were restricted to colonial territories in East Africa.

Since the effective date of transference of ministerial responsibility the Corporation's operations are financed from monies voted by Parliament, subject to effective control by the Secretary of State in consultation with the Treasury.

The Victoria League is a voluntary organization, founded in 1901 to promote individual friendship, knowledge and understanding among people of the Commonwealth, irrespective of their race, creed or political opinion. It has branches and correspondents throughout the Commonwealth, with a membership of over 25,000.

The principal activities are: arranging hospitality; administering hostels for Commonwealth students in London and Edinburgh; maintaining an Information Bureau in London for Commonwealth men and women students; acting as guardian, and by other means ensuring the welfare of Commonwealth students in the United Kingdom; arranging the distribution of gift food-parcels received from Victoria League branches overseas; despatching books and newspapers overseas; arranging lectures to schools and other organizations; linking schoolchildren by correspondence; and helping in any way they may need all visitors from the Empire overseas, who do not need to become subscribers in this country whether or not they are members.
The younger generation are looked after by the Young Contingent which operates at all branches and has an age limit of 18–30.
In Canada the Victoria League is affiliated with the Imperial Order Daughters of the Empire.

The Women's Corona Club was founded in 1937 by Lady Graeme Thomson for the purpose of holding an Annual Dinner in London for members on the same night as the dinner organised by the (men's) Club. In 1950 the aims and activities of the Club were extended.
The objects of the Club are :

(i) to be a link in the United Kingdom and overseas, between women who by their own or their husband's work have a common interest in the well-being of the Colonial territories and to be a means of fostering a tradition of voluntary social work ;
(ii) by the exchange and pooling of the experience of members in different territories, to provide help and information on all matters likely to be of interest to them ;
(iii) to provide opportunities for members to meet each other at social occasions and in such other ways as may be desired.

Membership is open to women members of the Colonial Service and the Colonial Office, past and present, and wives and widows of members of the Colonial Service and the Colonial Office, past and present.
The subscription for Annual Members is 7s. 6d. and for Life Members £3 3s. 0d.
The headquarters of the Club are in the Colonial Office, where callers and enquiries are welcomed.

A Bureau is run by the Club for the purpose of giving help and information to members about recommended private homes and Holiday Homes where the children of Colonial Service parents can be accommodated during the school holidays; addresses of residential nursery and pre-preparatory schools specialising in the care of children whose parents are abroad; and addresses of leave accommodation for families. An index is kept of members who are able to escort children when travelling across London, or able to take them out at school half-terms.

The Club collects information likely to be of interest to women about present conditions in individual territories. It also arranges programmes of talks and tea-parties for women going out to the Colonies for the first time, or being transferred to a new territory.
President—Lady (Samuel) Wilson.
Vice President—Lady (Thomas) Lloyd.

ADEN
ADEN COLONY
See pages 58–62 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

The Aden College is under construction but the total cost (including provision for sports fields and ground lay-out) is now estimated to be Rs.26,22,000, a large increase over the original estimate. It has now been decided to sanction the additional expenditure on the scheme.
The Technical College opened with two classes in January, 1951.
The British Council no longer maintains Institutes for men and women in the Colony.
Labour and Welfare

The Labour and Welfare Department in Aden commenced in a small way in 1950, under the District Commissioner. In April, 1951, it became a separate department. The small staff of a Labour and Welfare Officer, an Assistant Welfare Officer, a Labour Inspector and one clerk, are busily engaged in trying to implement the Ordinances regulating labour, and in particular in endeavouring to create better labour relations. The Labour Officer is also Factory Inspector for the Colony.
Recently improved labour legislation includes the compulsory reporting of industrial accidents, and the introduction for the first time in Aden of the 48-hour week for manual workers.

A re-organised and active Labour Advisory Board has been created, with representatives of employers and non-employers. As there are no Trade Unions in Aden, selection of representatives for wage earners presents a problem.

In social welfare the main efforts have been directed towards the children. Probation for juvenile offenders who have homes in Aden has met with marked success. The juvenile offender from the Yemen or the Protectorate who has made his way alone into the Colony presents an intractable problem.
The first Play Centre for children under 12 was opened in May, 1951, and the first Boys' Clubs started early in that year. Both these latter two activities are organised by voluntary societies.
A Social Welfare Advisory Council has been established, which meets regularly.
A committee has investigated the problem of the blind and reported to Government its recommendations.
COMMUNICATIONS
Shipping
A pontoon is now available at the Port to facilitate landing of passengers from big passenger liners.
Air

The landing-ground, Air Traffic Control Centre and Air-Sea Rescue Services are managed by the Royal Air Force. Since 1st September, 1950, a Civil Enclave has been established and a terminal building which is under construction, is expected to be completed by the end of 1951. The parking apron is also being extended to meet increasing traffic.
There are no internal colony services. Aden Airways, a subsidiary of B.O.A.C., operates scheduled services to Cairo, Nairobi, Addis Ababa and Sharjah and intends to introduce a service to Karachi.
Services to the Aden Protectorate are :—
(1) A weekly service to Mukeiras on the Audhali plateau, with frequent additional charter flights.
(2) A weekly service to Riyan and Qatn in the Hadhramaut.
(3) Charter flights to Beihan.
Other operators are :—
Ethiopian Air Lines who have daily schedules connecting Addis Ababa and Aden via Dire-Dawa and Djibouti ;
Air India International who operate Constellation aircraft on a weekly service between Bombay and Nairobi with stops at Karachi and Aden ;
Misair who have recently been granted traffic rights and operate with Vikings :—
(1) One service a week from Cairo via Khartoum and Asmara, and
(2) One service via Djeddah and Asmara ; and
Yemen Air Lines who make frequent non-scheduled flights between Taiz and Aden.
Approximately three hundred aircraft a month land at or take off from Khormaksar airport carrying an average of 1,600 passengers and 146,450 kgs. of freight.

ADEN PROTECTORATE
See pages 62–67 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
GENERAL DESCRIPTION
The main chiefs in the Eastern Aden Protectorate, who are in protective treaty relations with Her Majesty's Government are :—
The Qu'aiti State—His Highness the Sultan Sir Salih bin Ghalib Al Qu'aiti, K.C.M.G.
The Kathiri State—Sultan Hussein bin Ali bin Mansur Al Kathiri.
The Wahidi Sultanate of Balhaf—Sultan Nasr bin Abdullah Al Wahidi.
The Wahidi Sultanate of Bir Ali—Sultan Alwi bin Salih Al Wahidi.
The Mahri Sultanate of Qishn and Socotra—Sultan Ahmed bin Abdullah bin Essa bin Afrar.
A similar list of chiefs in the Western Aden Protectorate will be found on page 63 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
HISTORY

A treaty with His Majesty's Government was signed in April, 1949, by the Wahidi Sultan of Balhaf (E.A.P.) whereby the Sultan undertook to accept the advice of the Governor of Aden in all matters connected with the welfare and development of the territory of the Sultanate.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
In the Western Aden Protectorate the Abdali and Fadhli States have their own departments of education.

The principal sources of revenue in the Eastern Aden Protectorate are customs, licences and excise duties. In the Qu'aiti State over three lakhs are spent on the Armed Constabulary and police, nearly two lakhs on medical and public health, and nearly three lakhs on education. The State has also spent large sums from reserves for agricultural improvement, irrigation, building and repairing of dams and deflectors, etc. The Kathiri State has also contributed substantially towards agricultural development.
The main source of revenue in the States of the Western Aden Protectorate is customs.
CURRENCY AND BANKING

In the Western Aden Protectorate the Rupee was replaced by the East African Shilling, with effect from the 1st November, 1951. The Riyal (or Maria Theresa Dollar) is still used, its value fluctuating with the value of silver.

In the Eastern Aden Protectorate, up to 1944, the Maria Theresa Dollar was the currency used throughout the interior, but in that year the Rupee currency was finally adopted. The Rupee has been replaced by the East African Shilling with effect from the 1st November, 1951, but the Maria Theresa Dollars remain the normal form of currency among the Bedouins, and in the less developed areas.
There are no banking facilities in the Aden Protectorate.
TRADE, INDUSTRY AND AGRICULTURE

The control of agriculture and the development of the irrigation potential of the Abyan area of the Western Aden Protectorate is in the hands of a Board consisting of two members from the Lower Yafai State, two members from the Fadhli State and three officers appointed by the Governor. It is anticipated that the 1950–51 season will yield a crop of 6,000 bales of cotton.

In the Eastern Aden Protectorate the main industries are agriculture, weaving, dyeing, fishing, dhow building, lime burning, and sheep and camel breeding. The staple crops are wheat, millet, dhurra, dukhn, museibli, some barley, tobacco,

BAHAMAS
HISTORY
Delete the first two sentences under this heading on page 68 of The Colonial Office List 1951 and insert :—

“ San Salvador (Watling’s Island), so-called by Columbus when he made his landfall there in October, 1492, was known as Guanahani by the aboriginal Lucayan Indians and is one of the most easterly of the Bahamas. A few years after Columbus discovered the islands, the Lucayans were transported to work the mines in Hispaniola ”.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
To the list of institutions which provide higher education, “ St. John’s College ” should be added.
Broadcast Lessons. The teacher-training lectures and adult education programmes are now broadcast in the afternoons from 3.15 p.m. to 3.45 p.m. instead of in the evenings.
Information

The North American Regional Broadcasting Conference was duly held in Canada in 1949 and continued in the United States of America in 1950. It was agreed at this Conference that the Bahamas should retain its Class 1-A clear channel on the frequency of 1,540 kcs.
In August, 1950, the Bahamas station ZNS started commercial operation and increased its hours to 15 per day.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads

New Providence is served by an adequate road system maintained by the Public Works Department. All the main roads have been surfaced with asphalt, many of them being reconditioned by the R.A.F. during the War. In the Out Islands, Eleuthera has now a good main arterial road, asphalt-surfaced over most of its length, but in the other islands the roads are mainly rough farm tracks, except in the principal towns and settlements, where they are asphalt or concrete-surfaced. Maintenance in the Out Islands is carried out by the local Board of Works.
Shipping
Ships of the Canadian National Steamship Line now provide a regular freight service from Halifax or Montreal.

Freight service between the United Kingdom and the Colony is maintained by steamers of the Royal Mail Line and the Pacific Steam Navigation Company. The latter Line also provides a passenger service by the “ Reina del Pacifico ”. There is also a regular freight service between Nassau and New York and Florida. During the winter months, a number of cruise ships call at Nassau.
Air
Oakes’ Airport, the main airport in the Bahamas, provides 24-hour service for long and short range international and local airlines. During 1950 a considerable increase in air traffic
over previous years was evident and frequent services were operated between Nassau and England, Canada, the United States, British colonies in the Caribbean and South America.

British Overseas Airways provide services between England and Nassau via Bermuda, and between Nassau and South America via Jamaica. Both British Overseas Airways and Pan-American Airways provide daily services between Nassau and Miami with an increase in frequency of services during the winter season.

Trans-Canada Airlines operate frequent services between Nassau and Toronto, Montreal and Tampa. The Nassau-Montreal service connects with the trans-Atlantic routes of this company to Scotland, London and the continent of Europe. These services are also increased during the season.

The major local company, Bahamas Airways, provides frequent services to West Palm Beach via West End, Grand Bahama, and also conducts twice-weekly services to the main Out Islands. Aircraft of this company and another local company, Skyways Limited, are available for charter flights to any of the Out Islands.
Besides Oakes’ Airport, there is another airport at West End, Grand Bahama, which operates from dawn to dusk only, and a private landing strip at Rock Sound, Eleuthera.

At the end of 1950, there were thirteen aircraft on the Bahamas Register of Aircraft. During the year, Oakes’ Airport experienced an increase in traffic of approximately 28 per cent. over the 1949 movements. Traffic in the Nassau Control Area was also greatly increased due to the opening of Grand Bahama Airport.
Postal
Internal Services. The daily airmail service to West End, Grand Bahama, has been replaced by a service operating two to three times per week.
International Services. The despatch of airmails direct to Great Britain and Bermuda now takes place three to four times per week (as compared with five times per week previously).

BARBADOS
See pages 74–81 of The Colonial Office List 1951 .
GENERAL DESCRIPTION

In The Colonial Office List 1951 , page 74, it is stated that "In the north-west—the Scotland district—the watershed is nearer to the coast and the descent to the sea is consequently steeper." For "north-west" should be read "north-east".
CONSTITUTION
The Treasurer is no longer nominated by the House of Assembly.
In 1951 adult suffrage was introduced and the number of electors is expected to exceed 95,000.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

In addition to the scholarships and exhibitions tenable at Oxford or Cambridge University, Codrington College and the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture in Trinidad, two exhibitions are awarded for the University College of the West Indies.
Labour
The membership of the Barbados Workers' Union has increased to 12,000.
At the end of 1950 there were 100 Barbadian workers in the United States of America under contract and a total of 112 distributed among the islands of Bermuda, Curacao and Aruba.
COMMUNICATIONS
Shipping
The principal steamship lines operating at the end of 1950 were :—

Alcoa Steamship Company Incorporated, Blue Star Line, Canadian National Steamships, Compagnie Générale Trans-atlantique, Elders & Fyffes, Harrison Line, Furness Withy & Company, Limited, Moore McCormack Line, Royal Netherlands Steamship Company, and Saguenay Terminals Limited.
Barbados is the headquarters of the schooner trade in the Eastern Caribbean.
Air
The landing ground at Seawell has a new hard-surfaced runway of 6,000 feet.
Trans-Canada Airlines operate to and from Montreal weekly.
Telecommunications
With regard to internal telecommunications, the wire broadcasting system is provided by the Barbados Rediffusion Service Limited.
Posts
During 1950 1,185,580 postal packages were despatched and 1,135,706 received.
PUBLIC FINANCE
(See Note on page 27.)

Delete the figures given under Revenue and Expenditure for 1949–50 under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 and insert "Revenue—£1,997,299 and Expenditure—£2,150,813." For 1950–51 the Revenue amounted to £2,263,927 and the Expenditure to £2,008,892. The Public Debt at the 31st March, 1951, remained at £605,360. The Sinking Fund at that date was £276,601.
In 1949–50 grants and loans to the Colony under the United Kingdom Colonial Development and Welfare Acts amounted to £337,500. The figures for 1950–51 were £3,203.
Customs revenue for the financial year 1950–51 amounted to $4,664,404, an increase of $722,781 over the revenue for the previous year.
Duty collected on rum during the financial year was $934,742.
The amount of income tax collected during the financial year was $3,675,740, an increase of $818,739 on the amount collected in the previous year. The rates of income tax vary from year to year.

The main items of expenditure during the financial year 1950–51 were : Education, $1,651,566, an increase of $111,044 ; Medical Services, $1,036,729, an increase of $86,810 ; Roads and Transport, $626,684, a decrease of $107,009 ; Subsidies, Controls, etc., $1,159,452, an increase of $15,755.
CURRENCY AND BANKING

The currency system comprises notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board, set up in 1951, notes previously issued by the Government and by the Governments of British Guiana and Trinidad, and United Kingdom coin of which silver is legal tender up to $9.60 (£2). There is no local issue of coin. Government currency notes continue as legal tender but are being withdrawn with the introduction of the Eastern Caribbean note issue. With this issue the currency note system of the Eastern Group of British Caribbean Territories (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and the Windward Islands) was unified in 1951. Legislative provision was also made for eventual unification of coin.
The number of depositors of the Government Savings Bank at the 30th June, 1950, was 40,876, and the total amount standing to their credit was $10,471,968.

The trade figures for goods imported during 1950, which amounted to £8,065,944, showed an increase as compared with £7,064,988 for 1949. This increase was due chiefly to the importations of greater quantities of goods and the increased values. The chief imports were animal feeding-stuffs, fish (all kinds), flour, meat (all kinds), rice, lumber, bags and sacks (empty), cotton piece

BERMUDA
See pages 81–87 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
POPULATION
The total population, excluding service personnel, was estimated at 37,394 as a result of census returns at October, 1950. The figure for the white population was 13,497 and for the coloured, 23,897.
CONSTITUTION
The members of the Executive Council and the Legislature are now paid at the rate of 24s. for each day's attendance.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
The Board of Education now consists of a Chairman and ten members.
The present value of the Rhodes Scholarship is £368 per annum.
Health
More than half the adult population has had a chest X-ray. The incidence of active tuberculosis is about 1 in 1,500 in both the white and coloured communities and is falling satisfactorily.
V.D. has been under compulsory treatment since 1943 with good results and, with penicillin treatment, compulsion is seldom needed.
The King Edward VII Hospital (general) received a subsidy of £85,000 in 1950, the Nursing Home £6,000 and the Bermuda Welfare Society, £2,900.
The Department of Health maintain a school service and has planned parenthood, pre-natal and pre-school age clinics. Food-handlers are examined. Milk is pasteurised.
Mosquito control has been in force since 1942 with the practical elimination of aedes aegypti but culex fatigans still maintains strongholds.
Rat control has been started.
The infant mortality rate is 31.3 for the white and 45.2 for the coloured population per 1,000 births. The general death rate is 8.5 and 8.9 respectively per 1,000.
Labour
The "Teachers' Association of Bermuda" (26 members) has now been registered as a trade union. The membership of the other teachers' union has increased from 114 to 121.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads
No person may use a motor car which at the time of its importation was a used car.
Air

British Overseas Airways Corporation, Pan American World Airways and Colonial Airlines maintain regular shuttle services between Bermuda and the United States of America. British Overseas Airways Corporation also operate a twice-weekly service from London through Bermuda to Nassau, Jamaica and return. Trans-Canada Airlines operate a shuttle service weekly to Bermuda from Montreal and Toronto, extending one flight per week through to Barbados and Trinidad. Aerovias Guest, S.A., stop at Bermuda on their regular weekly service each way between Mexico City and Europe via Miami and the Azores. Iberia Airlines and Compania Cubanas also operate a weekly service across the mid-Atlantic route via Bermuda between Madrid and Havana, Cuba. Avianca flies twice a month east-bound only through Bermuda and the Azores to Europe, from Baranquilla, returning via the South Atlantic.
Cable and Wireless (West Indies) Limited

Direct radio telephone circuits are now operated with New York, Barbados and London and through these distant terminals long distance facilities are available with U.S.A., Canada, the United Kingdom, the British West Indies, and many other parts of the world.
Radio telephone services are now available with the T.S.S. "Ocean Monarch".
The aeronautical point-to-point services provided by the Company under contract with the Air Ministry and the Bermuda Government no longer cover Prestwick.

BRITISH GUIANA
BRITISH GUIANA
See pages 88–101 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
CONSTITUTION

A Constitutional Commission, composed of Sir John Waddington, G.B.E., K.C.M.G., K.C.V.O. (Chairman), Professor V. T. Harlow, M.A., D.Litt. and Dr. Rita Hinden, Ph.D., was appointed by the Secretary of State in October, 1950. The main recommendations of its Report, which was published on 19th October, 1951, as Colonial No. 280, were (i) universal adult suffrage (ii) an increased elected majority in the Legislature and (iii) ministerial responsibility for elected members. In a despatch published with the Report the Secretary of State accepted these proposals and also agreed in principle to a bicameral system for the Legislature. It is intended to introduce the new Constitution in 1953.
ADMINISTRATION
Local Government
The revenue of the Georgetown Town Council in 1950 was £316,222 and the expenditure, £276,390.
The revenue of New Amsterdam Town Council was £57,733 and the expenditure, £53,031.
The combined figures for revenue and expenditure of all local authorities in 1950 were £80,296 and £75,495, respectively.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
The total expenditure on administration and grants in respect of education in 1950 was £461,675.

Three exhibitions to the University College of the West Indies were awarded during 1950 in addition to the other scholarships and exhibitions indicated under this sub-heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 .

Evening classes were held at the Carnegie Trade School for Women, for women and girls of all ages who could not attend the day school but wished to improve their knowledge of cookery, dress-making, children's clothes, smocking and embroidery. These classes consisted of 12 sessions and 600 persons attended. The revenue of the Trade School in 1950 was £3,592 and the expenditure, £9,532.
Health
The accommodation at the Canje, Berbice, Mental Hospital has increased from 500 to 744 beds.

At present there are 94 registered practitioners in the Colony, of whom 45 are employed by Government (36 holding posts as G.M.O.s and 9 as administrative and specialist officers) and 40 registered dentists, including one full-time Government Dental Officer and two part-time Dental Officers. There are 534 Registered Nurses and 754 Registered Midwives.
Welfare

During 1950 investigations were conducted by the Cottage Industries Officer into a number of minor industries. The commercial production of a cereal confection from padi, "Padipops", was commenced in July, 1950, and 25,000 packets worth $2,000 odd were produced and sold locally.
There is an old age pension of $4.50 per month for those over 65.
The Youth Council referred to under this sub-heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 is an affiliate of the World Assembly of Youth.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads

A system of motor trails in the Rupununi District now connects the various airstrips with the Amerindian Settlements and ranchers' homesteads. Approximately 400 miles of trails in this area have led to an increasing use of motor transportation where, previously, horse and bullock-cart were the only means of transport. A series of bridges in the system now makes it possible to travel by motor transport from Annai, the north terminus, to Ishalton, the south terminus, a distance of approximately 210 miles.
Air

The British Guiana Airways Limited operates a network of weekly services to all Interior areas. The Company's fleet of Dakota (D.C.3) and Grumman Goose Amphibian aircraft is also available for charter flights internally to points in the Interior, or externally to the West Indies, Dutch and French Guiana, and the Federal Territory of the Rio Branco in North Brazil.

The Colony's International Airport is at Atkinson Field, 25 miles south of Georgetown. There are also airstrips at Mackenzie, Demerara River, and Mahdia in the Potaro, and Annai, Lethem and Wichabai in the Rupununi. In addition a network of satellite landing strips and an artificial water alighting area at Baramita in the North-West District have been constructed to provide quick air transportation for interior needs. A thrice weekly scheduled air service connects Bartica, a mining town at the confluence of the Essequibo and Mazaruni Rivers with Georgetown.
K.L.M. (Royal Dutch Airlines) operates a weekly service to and from Georgetown on Sundays.
PUBLIC FINANCE
(See Note on page 27.)

In 1950 the Revenue of the Colony amounted to £4,498,177 and the Expenditure to £4,912,691. (These figures include grants and interest-free loans from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds amounting to £229,833.)
The Public Debt amounted to £4,742,780.
Customs receipts in 1950 amounted to £1,852,566, and Income Tax receipts to £1,231,646.
No grants-in-aid of administration were received during the year.
Public Debt

No loans were raised or redemptions effected during 1950. The Colony's Funded Debt at 31st December, 1950, amounted to $23,765,343 (£4,951,113), exclusive of the liability in respect of the outstanding Railway Permanent Annuities and Perpetual Stock involving an annual charge of $84,378 (£17,579) but including an amount of $1,135,577 (£236,579) representing loans under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts which have been brought on charge.
The Sinking Fund for the redemption of the Public Debt totalled $2,725,174 (£567,745), the investments having a mean market value of $2,684,938 (£559,362) at the 31st December, 1950.

Customs Tariffs
The total amount of revenue collected from import duties in 1950, was £778,855 from goods paying specific duties and £619,697 from goods subject to ad valorem rates.
CURRENCY AND BANKING
The total circulation of all denominations of B. G. Government paper currency on the 31st December, 1950 was $9,431,000, or £1,964,792.
The total circulation at the 31st December, 1950, of bank notes issued by Barclays Bank (D.C. & O.) Limited, and the Royal Bank of Canada, was $741,670, or £154,515.
The circulation media now consist of :—

(a) Notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board established in 1951: notes previously issued by the Government and by the Governments of Barbados and Trinidad continue as legal tender but are being withdrawn. The note issuing powers of the local Banks have been terminated.

The currency note systems of the Eastern Group of the British Caribbean Territories (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and the Windward Islands) were unified in 1951; legislative provision was also made for eventual unification of coin.
Currency notes are of unlimited legal tender.
(b) British silver and bronze coinage, and a local 4d. bit. Silver and bronze coins are legal tender to amounts not exceeding 40s. and 1s. respectively.
Barclays Bank (Dominion, Colonial and Overseas), Limited, and the Royal Bank of Canada now
have branch establishments with savings bank departments at Mackenzie as well as those at Georgetown and New Amsterdam.
On the 31st December, 1950, Post Office branch banks had a total depositors' liability of $13,595,000, or £2,832,292.
PRODUCTION AND TRADE
Sugar and its by-products furnished 54 per cent. in value of the total domestic exports in 1950.
During 1950, 195,651 tons of sugar and 2,700,000 proof gallons of rum were produced. 52,600 tons of rice were produced.
Mining Operations in British Guiana
Production of gold for the year 1950 was 13,740 oz. of bullion, representing a decrease of 7,358 oz. on that for 1949.

The output from the British Guiana Goldfields Ltd. was 7,444 ounces from one dredge operating in the Mahdia, a tributary of the Potaro River. With money which the Colonial Development Corporation invested in this Company, a second-hand, six cubic foot, endless bucket dredge was purchased in the U.S.A. for use on the Company's concession in the lower Potaro River. It has been erected at Tumatumari but operations have been delayed through the late arrival of the power plant.

The output from Cuyuni Goldfields Ltd., 4,199 ounces of bullion, showed a decrease of 928 ounces of bullion on the previous year. This Company completed in October the salvage operation on the re-treatment of tailings left from their previous workings at Aurora Mine, which has now been placed on a care and maintenance basis.

The Tikwah Mining Corporation closed down their operations at Tikwah Mine in March and the personnel was transferred to Peters Mine on the Puruni River, where they are preparing to erect a plant to treat the old tailing dump by the cyanide process.

The Rupununi Gold Mining Company were forced in April to suspend operations temporarily due to exhaustion of capital. The mine remained on a care and maintenance basis and a small quantity of gold was produced by the Caretaker.
Messrs. Phang and Leung Bros., and Dragten and Baird were the chief producers in Barama, N.W.D., and produced 857 ounces for the year, a decrease of 1,754 ounces on their 1949 output.

The Anaconda British Guiana Mines, Limited, decided in August, 1950 to close down gold-prospecting operations and to cancel the options they had with local mining companies. The company had expended a sum of nearly $3,000,000 during the period of their operations.
Output of diamonds for 1950 was 37,462 metric carats, as against 34,790 in 1949, an increase of 2,672 carats.

It is not possible to ascribe the reason for the increase to any particular cause as there were no spectacular discoveries during the year. Prices fluctuated between $44.00 (£9 3s. 4d.) and $4600. (£9 11s. 8d.) per carat. It fell during mid-year to $35.00 per carat for small stones (£7 5s 10d).
The Mazaruni continues to be the main producer with 25,987.38 metric carats or approximately 75 per cent. of the Colony's production. The Potaro and Rupununi districts have shown notable increases.

BRITISH GUIANA
The Diamond and Metals Exploration Company Incorporated abandoned their attempt at diamond mining with a 4-inch Walberg Suction Dredge in the Eping Creek.

The total tonnage of bauxite exported in 1950 was 1,583,417 long tons as compared with 1,757,650 long tons in the previous year. Despite this decreased export the value increased to $13,832,442 as compared with $12,009,356 for 1949.

The Demerara Bauxite Company Limited continued to be the main producer with 1,461,653 tons. The Berbice Company Limited produced 121,764 tons. These two companies carried out extensive prospecting operations throughout the year and both have plans for expansion programmes on hand. It is hoped that exports will
be increased in 1951. The Demerara Bauxite Company have announced their intention to increase the calcining capacity of their plant at Mackenzie.

Plantation Bauxite Company Ltd., which is jointly owned by the Demerara Bauxite Company and the British and Colonial Bauxite Company (the latter a subsidiary of the British Aluminium Company Ltd.) has been carrying out preliminary surveys on the lands held by the Company under lease, in order to locate a site for a bridge across the Demerara River, so that the ore can be transported across the river for treatment at the Demerara Bauxite Company's plant at Mackenzie.

COMMUNICATIONS
Air

The air services listed under this sub-heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 continue to operate with the exception of Sevicio Aereo de Honduras S.A. (SASHA). The BWIA service to Jamaica, however, now operates only once weekly.

In addition, Caribbean International Airways (CIA) maintain a bi-weekly service from and to Grand Cayman, B.W.I. ; and a TACA International Airways service covers the route New Orleans–Belize–Guatemala and the return flight once a week.
Telecommunications facilities are provided for aviation services at Stanley Field.
Posts and Telegraphs
Telephone offices which also transact telegraph business have increased to 133.
As regards external communications maintained by wireless, the Tropical Radio Telegraph Company's stations concerned are now at Miam and Tegucigalpa.

Items of news interest and forecast for the Western Caribbean are broadcast daily at 12.30 p.m. and 7.15 p.m. from the Belize Broadcasting Station "Radio Belize", on a frequency of 4,950 Kilocycles. Storm and hurricane warnings are broadcast during the hurricane season from June to November.

In addition to the fact that the Turkish Family Law is expressly saved, the old Sher'i Courts which administered the Sher'iat (or Kuranic Law) were, on the recommendation of the Turkish Affairs Committee and as a result of the Turkish Family Courts Law, 1951, replaced by the Turkish Family Courts. Instead of being presided over
Digitized by Google
CYPRUS

by the Sher'ie Hakimi (or Kadi) the new Courts have, as Presidents, Turkish Family Court Judges, the old office of Kadi being abrogated. These Courts have jurisdiction over persons of the Moslem faith in matters concerning betrothal, marriage, divorce (and matters pertaining thereto), maintenance, registration of Vakfiehs (Moslem Charitable, or otherwise inalienable grants, or trusts) and administration of Infants' Estates. Appeals lie in the usual way to the Supreme Court. The Sher'i Laws previously in force were repealed by the Turkish Family (Marriage and Divorce) Law, 1951.

FALKLAND ISLANDS AND DEPENDENCIES
See pages 122–126 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
CLIMATE

The islands are in the same latitude South as London is North but there is little similarity in climate apart from hours of sunshine, which are almost identical. Mean monthly temperatures are uniformly lower but London has both higher and lower extremes. Warm spells, such as occur in a good English summer, are never experienced. Cold outbreaks may occur at almost any time of the year and the weather is generally changeable. The islands are exposed and persistent strong winds spoil many otherwise pleasant days in the summer. (The wind reaches gale force one day in five.) Annual rainfall is rather higher than in the London area.

The climate is bracing. Some people find the strong winds trying though somewhat similar conditions are found in the exposed coastal districts of Scotland. Spring, autumn and winter clothing, as used in the United Kingdom, is suitable. There is no need for extra-heavy under-clothes and wind-proof outer clothing is much warmer.
Mean annual temperature .. 43° F. Mean annual maximum .. 70° F. Mean annual minimum .. 19° F. Mean annual wind speed .. 15 knots Mean annual rainfall .. 30 inches

CONSTITUTION AND ADMINISTRATION

The Government is administered by a Governor, aided by an Executive Council composed of three ex officio members and two nominated members, and a Legislative Council composed of three ex officio members, four elected members, two nominated unofficial, and two nominated official members. The nominated members of the Legislative Council are appointed by warrant under the Royal Sign Manual and Signet. Unless the Council is dissolved earlier by the Governor, elections are held every four years.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

In Stanley a Government School is maintained from public funds. It provides infant, primary and secondary courses. The school-leaving age is 14, but provision is made for voluntary attendance at a continuation class for a two years' course of further education. This, in many subjects, reaches school certificate standard.
The Government contributes £2 per month towards the cost of board and lodging of children from the country districts attending the Stanley or Settlement Schools.

One Supervisor of Camp Education and ten Camp teachers are provided by the Government. Some of the teachers travel from house to house but seven settlements have resident teachers. The number of children receiving education in 1950 was 353.

Two scholarships, for a period of three years, are awarded annually to successful candidates between the ages of 12 and 14 years at schools in England. The first scholarships commenced in February, 1943.
(Note : "Camp" is the term applied locally to the country districts outside Stanley.)
Health

The Medical Service of the Colony is at present carried out by three Government Medical Officers, two in Stanley and one at Fox Bay on the West Falkland. In addition to this the Falkland Islands Company employs a doctor at Darwin on the East Falkland.

The Hospital in Stanley is in charge of the Senior Medical Officer. It has, at present, 17 beds, but is in process of extension. When the work is completed in a few months' time it will accommodate 35 patients.

Ante-natal and Child Welfare Clinics are well attended. The Hospital maintains a Maternity Department in which nearly all the confinements from both islands take place. Examinations of the school children take place twice a year and are combined with regular tuberculin tests.
A Dental Officer is stationed at Stanley and a Dentist for the outlying farms on East and West Falklands will, it is hoped, commence duty early in 1952.

The population, entirely European, is, generally speaking, of good health, but has, as frequently observed in other isolated communities, little resistance to infectious diseases imported from the South American coast, which is only five days journey by sea. Fortunately, no severe epidemic diseases of the notifiable group have occurred during the last five years.

The efficiency of the Medical Service, previously entirely dependent on communication by horse or boat, has greatly improved since the introduction of an Air Service, which has proved invaluable in cases of emergency.
Welfare

Although there is no special Welfare Department its purposes are served by the Medical Department, Stanley Town Council, and the Labour Federation. There is an old established Benefit Club in Stanley which provides a form of insurance against unemployment through sickness of its members and assists with funeral expenses. A scheme of Children's Allowances has been introduced by the Government.

SOCIAL SERVICES
Health

The Central Medical School in Suva now has a teaching staff of 3 whole time officers and 14 honorary lecturers. The whole time officers consist of the principal, assistant principal and a dental officer. The honorary lecturers include the medical and surgical specialists and other medical officers of the department. The school was founded in 1884 for the training of Fiji youths. Since 1928, however, its benefits have been available to other Western Pacific territories and its graduates, now numbering over 300, include Fijians, Fiji Indians, Western Samoans, Eastern Samoans, Tongans, Gilbertese, Ellice Islanders, Solomon Islanders, New Hebrideans, Rotumans, Nauruans, Cook Islanders and Niue Islanders. In January, 1951, 29 dental students and 37 medical students from the U.S. Trust Territory, Pacific Islands, who had been enrolled at the U.S. Naval Medical School at Guam were transferred to the Central Medical School in Suva. A larger central medical school and hostel to accommodate 150 students is being built.

Postal and Telegraphs

Overseas surface mails to New Zealand and United Kingdom via New Zealand are despatched at an average of three times each month and similarly to Australia and India, via Australia Mails to North and South America and United Kingdom via Canada are despatched approximately twice monthly. Airmails to United King-
dom, Near and Far East and Australia are despatched five times weekly and to New Zealand, North and South America and Hawaii, thrice weekly. Samoa is served weekly and the Cook Islands fortnightly.

Internal radio stations have been established at the following places :—Suva, Lautoka, Nayavu, Levuka, Moturiki, Makogai, Vunisea, Lambasai Savusavu, Nambouwalu, Mbutha Bay, Rambi Island, Waiyevo (Taveuni), Ndreketi, Natewa, Wainunu, Lomaloma, Rotuma, Lakeba, Matuku Island, Kambara Island, Mbenga Island, Mothe Island, Fulanga Island, Oneata Island, Koro Island and Ngau Island (fixed stations owned and operated by the Fiji Government) : M. V. "Andi Maopa", M.V. "Viti II", M.V. "Dengei" (Fiji Government ships) : Nandi Airport, Undu Point, Ono-I-Lau, Yasawa-I-Rara (N.Z. Meteorological Stations) : Wakaya Island, Katafanga Island, Mambavatu and Naitamba (privately-owned stations). Services are confined to circuits between Suva and other Government and private stations for public correspondence and Nandi Airport and meteorological stations for weather traffic, except in emergency. The following Fiji Government radio-telephone stations were to be in operation by the end of 1951 : Moala Island, Totoya Island, Vatoa Island, Nayau Island, Vanua Vatu Island, Namuka-I-Lau Island, Waimotu (Vanua Levu) and Vunindawa (Viti Levu).

SITUATION AND AREA
The total area of the Colony, excluding the parts administered as a Protectorate, is 29.4 square miles (not 69 square miles as stated in The Colonial Office List 1951 ).

CONSTITUTION

The Executive Council was revised during 1951 and, at the end of the year, consisted of the Governor as President, the Colonial Secretary ( ex Officio ), and five official and four unofficial members all of whom were nominated.
An amending Legislative Council Order-in-Council was made on the 30th June, 1951, and the changes are reflected in the new composition of the Council given below.

(b) Protectorate

COMMUNICATIONS

The airfield is at Yundum, distant 17 miles from Bathurst. It is controlled by the Government and operated on behalf of the Ministry of Civil Aviation by International Aeradio Ltd. The Public Works Department maintains runways, buildings, power and water supplies. The Meteorological Service is operated by the West African Meteorological Service. The regular scheduled service calling at Yundum is that of the

West African Airways Corporation which calls twice weekly on the route Lagos–Accra–Roberts Field–Freetown–Dakar. This service connects northwards at Dakar with the trunk routes between the United Kingdom, Europe and South America and southwards via Sierra Leone, Gold Coast and Nigeria, with South Africa. Yundum is also used as a major diversion airport for Dakar by the British Overseas Airways Corporation and Air France.

GENERAL DESCRIPTION
Gold Coast Colony

The Southern Togoland Territorial Council fulfils functions similar to those of the Joint Provincial Council of Chiefs in the Colony, in respect of the southern section of Togoland under United Kingdom Trusteeship which is administered with the Colony.
HISTORY
Constitutional History from the Nineteenth Century

In 1948 a Commission of Enquiry was appointed to investigate the underlying causes of certain disturbances which had occurred earlier in that year. The Commission's recommendations included various proposals for constitutional reform ; and as a result of His Majesty's Government's comments on the Commission's report, a fully representative all-African Committee was appointed to examine them. The Committee reported to the Governor in August, 1949 ; and after detailed study of its proposals a new constitution was drawn up which came into effect in January, 1951. The new constitution makes provision for a Legislative Assembly legislating for the whole of the Gold Coast and elected partly by constituencies and partly from the Territorial Councils of Chiefs. It also provides for an Executive Council of Ministers chosen from and approved by the Assembly. Three of the Ministers are officials. Certain reserve powers are retained by the Governor. The first elections, under a system of universal suffrage which was worked out for the constituencies, took place in February, 1951.
Economic and Social Developments

The social and economic history of the Gold Coast in the twentieth century has been accelerated by a number of influences and in particular by the spread of education and the development of the cocoa industry.
Digitized by Google
GOLD COAST

The prosperity of the various African companies for nearly four centuries depended in large measure on the slave trade. Its suppression in the nineteenth century, coinciding with the Ashanti wars, reduced commerce on the coast to straits from which it revived only temporarily when the security of Captain Maclean's administration fostered the development of the palm oil trade. Vegetable oils remained for several decades one of the principal objects of commerce on the coast. This and other trades were, however, displaced in importance by the cocoa industry which from small beginnings rapidly expanded in the new century until the Gold Coast became the world's largest producer, and cocoa the key to the country's prosperity. This prosperity is being threatened by swollen shoot, a serious and contagious virus disease of cocoa whose depredations constitute a grave menace to the economy of the country. A strenuous campaign based on the best scientific advice and research is being waged by Government and slow but steady progress is being made in the eradication of the diseased trees and the rehabilitation of the devastated areas. The marketing of cocoa has been undertaken since 1947 by the Government sponsored Gold Coast Cocoa Marketing Board.

From the days of the Portuguese, gold attracted to the coast adventurers of many nations; in recent times the prosperity of the Gold Coast has been increased by the discovery and working of diamonds, manganese and bauxite, and by the rapid expansion of the timber industry.
Takoradi Harbour, built during the Governorship of Sir Gordon Guggisberg and opened in 1928, has become the economic gateway of the country and contributes greatly to its prosperity and development.

The pioneers of education in the Gold Coast were the Wesleyan and Basel missions of the early nineteenth century. They were followed by many others, the Anglican, Presbyterian and Roman Catholic being prominent among them. Education was for many years left entirely to the missions, assisted in the latter part of the century by increasing subventions from Government. Throughout the present century Government, while continuing its aid to the missions, has also built many schools of its own, and Native Authorities have taken an increasingly prominent part in the extension of schooling facilities. Government has, in these and other ways, pursued a policy of wide educational expansion of which the opening of Achimota College in 1924 afforded remarkable evidence, and the establishment of the University College in 1948 a proof of a continued progress.
CONSTITUTION

The Constitutional instruments providing for the Government of the Gold Coast and setting up the constitution which came into effect on the 1st January, 1951, are the Gold Coast (Constitution) Order in Council, 1951; the Letters Patent, passed under the Great Seal of the Realm (constituting the office of Governor and Commander-in-Chief); the Northern Territories of the Gold Coast Order in Council, 1950; and the Instructions passed under the Royal Sign Manual and Signet to the Governor and Commander-in-Chief.
The Executive Council consists of the Governor as President; three ex officio members, namely the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General and the Financial Secretary; and not less than eight
representative members, who are members of the Legislative Assembly proposed by the Governor and approved by the Assembly.
The Leader of the Government Business in the Legislative Assembly is elected by a majority of the Executive Council from among their number.

The Governor at his discretion may charge any member of the Executive Council with the responsibility of Government Departments. Members are known as Ministers. There is provision for Ministerial Secretaries to be appointed from among members of the Legislative Assembly and for a Permanent Secretary in each Ministry who will be a public officer appointed by the Governor.

The Legislative Assembly legislates for all Territories of the Gold Coast and consists of a Speaker, three ex officio members, namely the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General and the Financial Secretary, three special members who represent the commercial interests of whom only one is permitted to vote, and three special members who represent mining interests also with only one vote. The seventy-five elected members are made up of thirty-three rural members, five municipal members, eleven territorial members elected by the Joint Provincial Council, six territorial members elected by the Asanteman Council, nineteen territorial members elected by the Electoral College of the Northern Territories formed out of the Northern Territories Territorial Council and one territorial member elected by the Southern Togoland Council.

The rural members are those elected by the people of constituencies whose boundaries were laid down in the Elections Ordinance, 1950, made under a special Order in Council for the purpose. This Ordinance laid down electoral procedure, of which the main provision is that elections in rural constituencies should take place by secret ballot in two stages.

In the first stage primary elections are held to choose representatives for local electoral colleges, which in the second stage choose one, or in certain cases two, representatives for each constituency. In order to qualify for registration to vote or for nomination as a candidate, an individual man or woman must be aged 21, a British subject and have a residential qualification of a minimum of six months. Literacy is not imposed as a qualification except in the case of candidates for the secondary elections.
The four municipalities of Accra, Kumasi, Sekondi/Takoradi and Cape Coast are represented by the urban members who are elected by direct election.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
Primary education consists of a six-year course from which pupils may proceed further to a four-year senior primary course.

Secondary Education. In December, 1950, 426 candidates from Government-assisted institutions, including the Achimota School, passed the overseas School Certificate Examination of the University of Cambridge Local Examination Syndicate.
The University College of the Gold Coast. There were 182 students taking Intermediate and Degree Courses and 28 taking the Associate Course at the Institute of Education during the 1950-51 session.
The College of Technology, Science and Arts, Kumasi. The Ordinance to establish this College was passed in September, 1951; and some
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

students were expected to be in residence in January, 1952. The college will play an important part in the structure of education in the Gold Coast and will eventually have an enrolment of 2,000 students in residence. It will provide courses in engineering, agriculture, commercial subjects, medical auxiliary services, teacher-training in specialist subjects, and in other professions at post-secondary levels.
Information Services
The Public Relations Department is now organised in seven sections : Administrative, Publications, Programmes, Broadcast Engineering, Cinema, Films and Photographic.

The Broadcast Engineering Section is responsible for the efficient running and maintenance of 21 Wire Rediffusion Stations and the relaying of B.B.C. and Gold Coast programmes to over 10,000 subscribers.
The Gold Coast Broadcasting Station broadcasts daily except Sundays and Public Holidays between 10.30 a.m. and 12 noon and between 3.15 p.m. and 6 p.m., on 49.6 and 61.04 metres respectively.

The purpose of the Films Section (the Gold Coast Film Unit) is to focus interest, through films, on matters of national concern and to project the Gold Coast to the outside world. "Amenu's Child", one of the films made in 1950, won an award at the Venice International Film Festival. During 1951 the Unit produced three documentary films, the subjects being clean water supply, Post Office savings facilities and juvenile delinquency.
Medical Facilities

Shortage of Medical Officers is still acute, but the position has been somewhat eased by an amendment to the Medical Practitioners' and Dentists' Registration Ordinance whereby temporary licences can be issued to doctors with registrable qualifications to practise in localities where their services are needed.
Out-patients treated in Gold Coast hospitals in 1950 numbered 947,231 ; 734 major operations were performed at the Gold Coast Hospital in Accra.
The new Midwifery Training School at Kumasi now accommodates 32 pupil midwives.

The Nurses Training College at Accra is functioning well. In 1950, 14 student nurses passed the State Registration Examination, making the total number of passes since the commencement of the examination, 28.

Of the three proposed rural health centres the one at Kpandu has been completed and a Senior Health Sister posted there. The sites have been acquired and contracts placed for a second centre to be constructed at Effiduasi in Ashanti and a third at Bimbilla in the Northern Territories.

The Medical Field Unit which is being organised into mass treatment teams is proving very useful. Its main function is to combat major endemic diseases such as yaws and trypanosomiasis and epidemic diseases such as cerebro-spinal meningitis and small-pox wherever possible. There are three mobile dispensaries under the charge of Government-trained nurses based on Winneba, Sunyani and Tamale respectively. These dispensaries which are an experiment are proving rather costly owing to the bad roads over which they travel and the frequent breakdowns resulting therefrom.
Health
In the rural areas particular attention is being paid by the Public Health authorities to village layouts, the protection of water supplies and the disposal of refuse.
Labour
An employment exchange has now been opened in Tamale, in addition to those at Accra, Kumasi, and Takoradi.
The senior staff of the Labour Department has been increased.
On the 1st April, 1951, there were 73 registered trade unions whose total paid-up membership was 23,726. This compares with 64 unions and 17,985 members on the 1st April, 1950.
Rural Water Development
To the end of April, 1951, 1,210 wells, 10 reservoirs, and 10 bore-holes had been completed in rural areas and in addition major piped supplies to seven towns were completed or in hand.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads
The mileage of all-weather roads maintained by the Public Works Department is now 3,685 miles, and that of roads maintained by the Political Administration, 2,928.
Railways
The gross earnings of the Railway and Takoradi and Accra Harbour Administration for the year 1950-51 were £3,262,611.
Air

The Gold Coast is connected with the United Kingdom by British Overseas Airways Corporation, with the United States of America and South Africa by Pan-American World Airways and with France, Portugal and Spain by airlines of the respective countries. West African Airways Corporation, besides operating an internal service, operate a coastal service to Dakar, where connection can be made with South America by B.O.A.C. and other international airlines. A W.A.A.C. service also operates to the Anglo-Egyptian Sudan.

Accra Airport, situated four-and-a-half miles north-east of Accra, is the Gold Coast international airport and is the terminus of the B.O.A.C. London-British West Africa service. It also handles P.A.W.A. aircraft operating between New York and Johannesburg, Air France aircraft operating between Dakar and Brazzaville, Transportes Aereos Portugueses aircraft operating between Lisbon and Lourenço Marques and, occasionally, Aviacion y Comercio aircraft operating between Madrid and Fernando Po or Bata.

There are three other aerodromes—at Takoradi, Kumasi and Tamale—and internal services to them by W.A.A.C. from Accra connect the western Gold Coast, Ashanti and the Northern Territories to the capital.
The four aerodromes have all-weather tarmac runways.
Posts and Telegraphs
There is now also a direct air parcel post service between the United Kingdom and the Gold Coast.
There are aeronautical radio-telecommunication stations with radio-beacons at Accra, Takoradi, Kumasi, and Tamale, with VHF and HF cathode-ray direction-finders at Tamale.
