HONG KONG
See pages 174–187 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
SITUATION AND AREA

The Colony of Hong Kong, consisting of a number of islands and a portion of the mainland on the south-eastern coast of China, is situated at the eastern side of the Pearl River between $22^\circ 9'$ and $22^\circ 37'$ N. and $113^\circ 52'$ and $114^\circ 30'$ E. The capital city, Victoria, situated on the island of Hong Kong, is 91 miles S.E. of Canton and 40 miles E. of the Portuguese Colony of Macao. The island of Hong Kong is about 11 miles long and from 2 to 5 miles broad (total area 32 sq. miles). Opposite and to the north of the island is the peninsula of Kowloon ( $3\frac{1}{2}$ sq. miles) and Stonecutters Island ( $\frac{1}{4}$ sq. mile) which, with Green Island and Aplichau, form the Colonial territory formally ceded by China. The other component of the Colony is the New Territories which includes a large number of islands and a portion of the mainland stretching north of Kowloon for approximately 16 miles, the boundary following the small Shumchun river. This territory is leased from China, its area being 359 sq. miles, making the total area of the Colony 391 sq. miles.
GENERAL DESCRIPTION

Hong Kong Island lies close to the mainland, being separated at one point by a narrow strait (Lyemun) not more than $\frac{1}{4}$-mile wide, forming the harbour's main deep-water approach used by ocean-going ships. The harbour (17 sq. miles water area) lies between the island and the mainland. It has one of the finest natural harbours in the world and has up-to-date dock facilities for the largest ships. The surrounding land consists of steep granite hills, which though in many parts treeless, form a panorama of outstanding beauty. The city of Victoria lies along the north side of Hong Kong Island facing the harbour and is largely built on a long strip of reclaimed land. In some places it has been possible to build houses

in terraces rising several hundred feet up the hillside and thanks to excellent roads and a vehicular railway there is a considerable residential area on and around the island's heights. The highest point on the island is Victoria Peak (1,809 ft.)

Kowloon, with a population equalling that of Victoria, lies on the mainland opposite Hong Kong on a narrow peninsula jutting southward into the harbour. It formerly was covered by low hills which have been extensively levelled and built on. It also contains the Colony's main industrial area, the main wharves for ocean-going ships, one of the two large dockyards with ship-building facilities, and the southern terminus of the Kowloon-Canton Railway which occupies the extreme southern tip of the peninsula. On the reclaimed area north-east of Kowloon lies the Colony's aerodrome, Kai Tak, with a seaplane base in front of it in Kowloon Bay. North of Kowloon lies the steep escarpment of the Kowloon hills under which the railway passes in a tunnel 2,404 yds. long. The most northerly part of Kowloon immediately south of the Kowloon hills forms one of the Colony's most attractive suburbs known as Kowloon Tong.

North of Kowloon and geographically separated from it by the escarpment, lies the main part of the New Territories by far the largest part of which consist of steep and barren hillside. Approximately in the centre of the area rises the Colony's highest mountain, Taimoshan (3,141 ft.). The eastern side of the New Territories is more mountainous than the west and has a deeply indented coastline along which are a few villages where a frugal living can be made by farming in narrow valleys and fishing in innumerable inlets. In the north-western part of the New Territories lies the main area of cultivable land in which lies the large market town of Yuen Long. This area, producing chiefly rice, stretches away on the north to the marshes on the eastern shore of Deep Bay. Also included in the New Territories are 75 islands, some of them uninhabited and others with no more than a single village consisting of one or two families. The largest is Lantao Island at the mouth of the Pearl River estuary, a rugged and beautiful place with twin peaks over 3,000 feet high. Round its coasts are several fishing villages and sheltered among its hills are a number of Buddhist monasteries and settlements. The most populous island is Cheung Chau, a local fishing centre with a land population of approximately 19,000.
POPULATION

No official census has been taken since 1931 when the total population was found to be 849,751. From 1937, when the Japanese invaded China, the Colony's population has fluctuated with the different changes in its own and China's fortunes. In 1941, due to the number of refugees from China, the population was estimated to have risen to over 1,600,000 but during the Japanese occupation of Hong Kong it fell rapidly until in August 1945 it was estimated to have been less than 600,000. After the war there was a steady influx of people, most of them Hong Kong residents returning from China, and to these were added from 1949 onwards a great number of refugees, largely from Shanghai, seeking to avoid the troubled conditions attendant upon the change of government in China. By May 1950 the population was estimated to have been the highest in the Colony's history—2,360,000—but in the second part of the year a decline began, many
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

of the refugees either proceeding overseas or returning to their homes in China. At the beginning of 1951 the population estimate was 2,030,000, and by the middle of the year it was evident that the figure was steadying at slightly below this last estimate.
ADMINISTRATION
Radio Hong Kong is now controlled by the Public Relations Office instead of by the Post Office as in the past.

The departments dealing with agriculture, fisheries, forestry and public gardens were combined in 1950 under a Director of Agriculture, Fisheries and Forestry. A Director of Marketing was appointed to control the government wholesale fish and vegetable markets; this officer is also Registrar of Co-operative Societies.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
In June 1951 there were 967 registered schools with a total enrolment of approximately 173,000 that is, 43,000 more than in June, 1949.

Primary education extends over a six years' course at the end of which selected pupils may enter either a five years' secondary course in English, leading to the Hong Kong School Certificate, or a six years' secondary course in Chinese which will in the near future lead to a Chinese School Certificate. Alternatively, at the end of the primary stage, pupils may enter the Government Junior Technical School or various private vocational schools. Certain schools have special post-school certificate classes giving a one years' course in preparation either for the Hong Kong University Matriculation or for business.
In the fifth paragraph under this sub-heading on p. 177 of The Colonial Office List 1951 reference is made to "election" to training colleges. The word "election" should be replaced by "entry".
A School for Higher Chinese Studies was opened in 1951 offering 3-year post-secondary school courses in Chinese, commerce and journalism.

The buildings of the University of Hong Kong were severely damaged and most of the equipment lost during the Pacific War. The University re-opened in 1946 since when the number of students has steadily increased. There are now over 700 students in the faculties of medicine, arts, science, engineering and architecture.
Health
The Hong Kong Anti-Tuberculosis Association's Ruttonjee Sanatorium now has approximately 130 beds.
On 30th June, 1951, the staff of the Medical Department of the Colony consisted of :—
Doctors, 156, of whom 132 were Chinese.
Nurses and Hospital Dressers, 579, of whom 478 were Chinese.
Health and Malaria Inspectors, 22, of whom 19 were Chinese.
Others (including technicians, subordinate and menial staff), 1,975, mostly Chinese.
Labour
The Commissioner of Labour, who is the adviser to Government on all labour matters, is assisted by a Labour Advisory Board on which employers and workers are represented in equal numbers.
The Illegal Strikes and Lock-outs Ordinance, 1949 (No. 16 of 1949) has been extended to the end of 1951.
Social Welfare

Unrestricted immigration until late 1950, a grossly overcrowded population, local sensitivity to political and military events elsewhere and also to the effects of natural disasters in nearby China and the fact that the majority of Hong Kong residents do not think of Hong Kong as their real home, increases the problems of constructive social welfare work.

A great deal of the Colony's social welfare and charitable work is carried out by over sixty voluntary organisations. Government provides heavy indirect subventions to many of these and in addition makes cash grants to nearly thirty of them through the Social Welfare Office.
The Government Social Welfare Office was established in August, 1947, as a specialised sub-department of the Secretariat for Chinese Affairs. Its principal responsibilities are :—
(a) the Probation Service, which includes the development of remand homes, probation hostels and approved schools ;
(b) public assistance work, which includes relief in cash or kind, the administration of welfare centres, emergency relief work after fires and typhoons, relief camps and repatriation;
(c) child welfare, which includes liaison with orphanages, home visiting, care and protection of deprived children, and liaison with voluntary organizations ;
(d) liaison with all voluntary youth welfare organizations and boys' and girls' clubs, and certain aspects of vocational training ;
(e) promotion of community welfare through urban neighbourhood associations ;
(f) moral welfare work with special attention to juvenile prostitution ;
(g) training of social workers, and the welfare of seamen, displaced persons, the aged and attempted suicides.
COMMUNICATIONS
Internal Communications

Through passenger services to Canton were suspended in October, 1949, after the change of administration in Canton and have not yet been resumed although goods trains run between the Colony and Shanghai.

The total number of passengers carried for the year ending 31st March, 1951, on the British Section of the Kowloon-Canton Railway amounted to 6,055,398. Goods tonnage totalled 371,790 tons. The gross receipts amounted to $10,366,797.
There are efficient taxi services, rickshaws and a few remaining sedan chairs in the Colony.
Shipping

Since 1945 the harbour has been almost entirely cleared of wrecks ; and the lights, moorings, wharfage and dockyard facilities have been restored to their pre-war efficiency. Hong Kong's record for rapid cargo-handling and quick turn round of shipping compares favourably with that of any other port in the Far East.
Owing to unsettled political conditions in China, the coastal trade from Hong Kong represents only a fraction of the tonnage engaged on this service
Digitized by Google
HONG KONG
before the war, and those same conditions have caused the disruption of the trade to Canton and other river ports.

The revival of the ocean trade to and from Hong Kong since the end of the war has been remarkable, and successive years have shown increases up till 1951. Although the tonnage figures for ocean-going vessels show a slight decline during the last year, the cargo which these vessels loaded and discharged was 496,935 tons greater than the previous year. The past year has also shown a remarkable increase in the tonnage of junks and launches using the port.
The net tonnages of vessels entered and cleared for the year ending 31st March, 1951, were as follows :—

Ocean-going vessels 17,773,362
River steamers .. 4,655,428
Junks and launches 4,415,556
Total .. 26,844,346
Air

In the year ending March, 1951, 73,064 passengers used the Airport and 5,290 aircraft arrived or departed on international flights. The airfield provides two runways, one of which has been extended to constitute the maximum length of 5,418 feet ; both are surfaced with asphalt on a concrete base. The largest aircraft using it are the Douglas DC-6. Canadairs and Constellations call regularly on scheduled air services. The adjacent marine base is suitable for all types of flying-boats.

A survey party from the United Kingdom Ministry of Civil Aviation has recently been studying the possibility of improving the present airport to enable it to cater for larger aircraft by the extension of existing, and construction of new, runways.
Telecommunications and Telephones
An extension of the radioplane network to cover South Korea, Singapore, Australia, New Zealand and North Borneo is projected.
New and up-to-date broadcasting studios are now situated on the top floor of Electra House (Cable and Wireless).
PUBLIC FINANCE
(See Note on page 27.)

For the financial year 1950–51 the Revenue of the Colony was $291,599,484 and the Expenditure, $251,677,495. Duties, Earnings and Profits Tax and Assessed Taxes (Rates) accounted for a revenue of approximately $147½ million (£9,250,000) in 1950–51.
Public Debt
The Public Debt of the Colony as at 31st March, 1951, totalled $64,632,000.
TRADE
(See Note on page 27.)
In 1950 the value of the Colony's imports was $3,787,700,000 and that of its exports was $3,715,600,000.
The volume of trade in 1950 thus showed an increase of over 48 per cent. as compared with 1949 and is a record in the Colony's history.
This increase is due in part to the disposal from congested godowns of goods which had been diverted to Hong Kong by reason of the unsettled
conditions in China in 1949. In addition the figures, which relate to value only, must be discounted to some extent in view of the rise in prices of both raw materials and finished goods.
Trade with the United Kingdom increased by 8.6 per cent. as compared with 1949.
CURRENCY AND BANKING
Government issues of coins comprise coins of 50, 10 and 5 cents in nickel brass.

JAMAICA
See pages 187–206 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
HURRICANE DAMAGE AND RECONSTRUCTION

Jamaica was struck by a hurricane on the night of the 17th August, 1951. 157 lives were lost. The most serious damage was in the South-East part of the Island but damage to agriculture was widespread. Banana trees throughout the Island were blown down. The Governor opened a Hurricane Relief Fund to which His Late Majesty King George VI and other members of the Royal Family made contributions. Amongst donations from many parts of the world was a gift of £250,000 from the U.K. Government. Towards the cost of reconstructing the Island's productive capacity, the U.K. Government agreed to make available up to £4,600,000 (up to £3,100,000 as a grant and up to £1,500,000 as a loan).

The salaries of the Speaker and Members of the House of Representatives have been increased from £1,000 and £650 per annum to £1,300 and £850 per annum, respectively, in addition to travelling and subsistence allowance.

COMMUNICATIONS
Air
A total of 6,680 flights were made in 1950 through the Palisadoes Airport.
The Montego Bay Airport was opened to internal traffic and limited international services throughout the year. There were 1,064 flights.
Grand Cayman : Alighting areas for flying boat operation were maintained at Grand Cayman and Cayman Brac ; the latter, however, can only be used when the prevailing wind is from the south.
Turks Island : The small landing strip was used by light commercial and private aircraft operating occasional services between the Island and the Dominican Republic.
South Caicos : The landing strip was available to all aircraft on request or for purposes of emergency, and was used on several occasions.

The Kingston Oceanic Air Traffic Control Centre and the 7th Regional Notam Centre, which were established at Palisadoes in April, 1948 (on which date International Civil Aviation Organisation procedures were implemented in the Caribbean), provided air traffic control services to all aircraft within or passing through the area.
The Centre, in 1950, controlled 15,494 flights, which was an increase of 1,804 flights over the year 1949.
On the 1st October, 1950, the boundaries of the control area were extended, thus increasing considerably the area controlled by Kingston.
During 1950, all International Civil Aviation Organisation requirements were fulfilled by the Centre and the Tower. The Tower handled 13,214 operations in that year.
The Government Aeronautical Radio Station (MRX), which provided air-to-ground, point-to-point and meteorological communication services, was handed over to Messrs. International
Aeradio (Caribbean) Limited on the 1st of July, 1950, and has been operated by that Company for and on behalf of Government since that date.
PUBLIC FINANCE (See Note on page 27.)

At the end of the financial year 1949-50, the public debt stood at £14,001,129 representing approximately £9 17s. per head of population, and the estimated value of the sinking funds accumulated towards the redemption of this debt was £2,304,797. The net debt was therefore approximately £11,696,332.

The main sources of revenue are customs and excise duties and internal revenue. The total revenue from customs duties in 1949-50 was £3,405,316 of which £3,309,554 was the yield from import duties. Of the £1,959,525 paid in excise duty in 1949-50, £700,684 was in respect of the duty on rum and £948,625 on tobacco. Internal revenue in 1949-50 included £2,001,832 from income tax and surtax, £31,929 from excess profits tax, £438,903 from postal charges and £120,135 from licences.
The Revenue and Expenditure for the financial year 1950-51 were £10,356,594 and £10,491,757 respectively.
Grants and loans under Colonial Development and Welfare Acts amounted to £1,800.
PRODUCTION AND TRADE (See Note on page 27.)
The value of the total trade of Jamaica showed a slight upward trend in 1950. Food, drink and tobacco accounted for £5,375,156 as against £4,807,125 in 1949.

The total value of commodity trade (imports and exports) for the year 1950 amounted to £37,487,023 of which imports accounted for £22,379,233, domestic exports, £14,662,696 and foreign exports, £445,094.

COMMUNICATIONS

A regular three-weekly steamship service is maintained from Georgetown with Kingston, Jamaica and Belize, British Honduras. There is also communication by sailing and motor vessels with the ports of Central America and the United States.
A weekly air service is operated between Jamaica, Grand Cayman and Tampa, Florida.
Alighting areas for flying-boat operation are maintained at Grand Cayman and Cayman Brac; the latter however, can only be used when the prevailing wind is from the south.
The deferred rate for wireless messages to the United States of America is now 5d. per word.

TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
GENERAL DESCRIPTION

Plans for the reorganisation of the solar evaporated salt industry have been mooted at various times for the last century, but from 1946 onwards great efforts have been made to find a solution to the difficulties of a small industry which is compelled to sell its products on a highly competitive world market, which had over fifty owners all with rival theories of what should be done and which was crippled by lack of capital. On April 9th, 1951, the Turks Islands Salt Company Limited took over the industry, the owners of salinas and ancillary equipment and property

voluntarily accepting £100,000 (including a schedule of Government equipment and property valued at £6,000) as compensation. Approximately one-third of the compensation was in cash and two-thirds in redeemable preference shares in the new Company. The new capital was made available from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds and a further sum of £60,000 is being loaned to the Company by the Colonial Development Corporation. The Government of the Dependency holds all the Ordinary shares in the Company and has a majority holding of the entire share capital and a majority on the Board of Directors. This variation of the principle of public ownership provides for a partnership of Public funds and Private capital and gives the Dependency the opportunity to acquire its major industry outright over a period of thirty years. The strong Welfare aspect of this scheme is balanced by the necessity for sound commercial management because of the highly competitive nature of the salt business. The tasks of re-organisation and of speeding up lightering and loading arrangements are now being tackled.

Post-War Reorganisation

The full title of the Chief Secretary is now "Chief Secretary and Member for Development". Other revised titles are :—The Deputy Chief Secretary and Member for Education and Labour, The Member for Agriculture and Natural Resources, and The Member for Health, Lands and Local Government. The Member for Commerce and Industry now sits on the Executive and Legislative Councils as an ex officio member.

Sir Reginald Edwin Robins is no longer a member of the Authority for Development and Reconstruction. The members now are Sir Godfrey Rhodes, C.B., C.B.E., D.S.O., M.L.C., Major A. G. Keyser, D.S.O., M.L.C. and Sir Alfred Vincent, M.C.A., in addition, of course, to the Chief Secretary and Member for Development as Chairman.

The Planning Committee set up in 1948 to revise the Development Programme, was reconstituted as a Standing Planning Committee in March, 1950, with the Chief Secretary as Chairman, two other official and four unofficial Members. This Committee submitted an interim report in November, 1950, and it presented in June, 1951, a comprehensive report recommending the lines of development for the remainder of the ten-year period.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Information Services

The African Information Services, set up at the beginning of 1951, are charged with the task of providing a centralized service for the production and employment of information material in all media designed to assist the Press Officer, District Teams and the departments of Government, so as to advance the social and economic welfare of the African peoples.
The following are the main services provided by the office :—
Press Office
The Press Office was established in the Secretariat on June 1st, 1951. All official hand-outs and communiques are issued to the European, Asian
Digitized by Google
C 2
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
and African Press through this office, and particular attention is now being paid towards improving the standard of the African vernacular press.

Articles on the work of Government Departments and matters of general interest are prepared for local press use and for overseas, and distribution is made through the office to the local press of material prepared by the Colonial Office and the Central Office of Information.

A summary of opinion on African affairs as expressed in the local press is prepared fortnightly and distributed for information only on a limited distribution list to those interested in African Affairs.
This office also prepares and issues the K.I.O. Fortnightly, a publication of particular interest to farmers.
Every assistance is rendered to overseas journalists visiting Kenya as well as to the press throughout the world when information on the Colony is required.
Publications
“Pamoja”, a Swahili broadsheet for Africans containing an editorial on local or world affairs, is published weekly and 18,500 copies distributed.
Funds and material are supplied for 14 district news sheets in local vernaculars and in some cases these are edited in the office.
Books, pamphlets, leaflets and posters are prepared as required to support specific publicity campaigns.
Broadcasting

Broadcasts to Africans are given daily from a studio in the office and transmitted throughout Kenya by Messrs. Cable and Wireless, Ltd. Special broadcasts such as running commentaries on the more important football matches and athletic meetings are given periodically as well as regular quiz competitions, radio plays and other features.
Three local broadcasts in Swahili are given each week from a studio in Mombasa. Funds for this service and material for the broadcasts are provided by the office.

The office owns and maintains some hundred wireless sets which are distributed to community centres, information rooms, district headquarters, missions, schools, etc., for communal listening to its programmes by Africans.
Still Photographs
A Photographic Section is maintained to provide pictures needed for the illustration of the Services' publications and to prepare filmstrips for Africans.
All photographs in a library of some 12,000 prints are available to the general public, visiting journalists, etc.
Prints are supplied to Government Departments for displays at Agricultural Shows and other exhibitions and to community centres for wall display.

The office owns and maintains some 90 filmstrip projectors which are placed in community centres throughout the Colony. In addition to locally produced filmstrips these are supplied with strips obtained from the United Kingdom.
Cine Films
A Production Unit makes 16mm. educational and comic films and newsreels for Africans.

A central film library is maintained consisting of locally produced films together with those supplied by the Central Office of Information and the Colonial Film Unit and some purchased from commercial sources.
In addition to the mobile information units this library supplies some 105 projection posts showing to Africans throughout the Colony.
35mm. copies of "British News" received from the Central Office of Information are supplied to commercial cinemas.
Mobile Information Units

Seven Mobile Information Units are attached to Provincial Commissioners' offices in the four main provinces. They are constantly on safari, working under the direction of the Provincial Administration, and give cinema shows in the evenings, and lectures supported by filmstrip shows during the daytime. The majority of the shows are for Africans but European and Asian schools also receive visits from time to time.
Despatch and Distribution

In addition to its own publications the office distributes some 10,000 copies of each issue of the Colonial Office publication in Swahili and English, "Siku Hizi", free to individual Africans, community centres, schools, information rooms, district headquarters, missions and employers of large labour forces. Posters, booklets, etc., received from the Colonial Office and the Central Office of Information are also distributed to various lists of addressees in the Colony, selected according to the type of material.
The average weekly output amounts to some 35,000 items.
Translation Work
The office undertakes translation work for other Government departments in five local vernaculars.
Health
Local Native Councils are now known as African District Councils.
Welfare

The Social Welfare Organisation has been renamed The Community Development Organisation. It is still, however, a branch of the African Affairs Group. It has its Headquarters at Jeanes School, Kabete, where the Central Composite Training Centre is also situated.

The Commissioner for Social Welfare has been re-designated the Commissioner for Community Development. He is directly responsible to the Chief Native Commissioner for the policy with regard to Community Development including African Information Services and the Jeanes School.

In general the word "Welfare", previously occurring in the designation of officials dealing with community development, has been replaced by the words "Community Development"—e.g. African Welfare Workers are now known as Community Development Assistants.
Remedial welfare work is now organised separately.

COMMUNICATIONS Roads
The Colony's road system consists of approximately 17,000 miles of road maintained as follows :—
4,680 by Public Works Department.
2,628 by European District Councils.
272 by Municipal Councils.
481 by Royal National Parks Trustees.
477 by Forestry Department.
8,500 by African District Councils and the Administration.
237 miles of the P.W.D. roads system are bitumen surfaced.

The great North road passes through Kenya, entering the Colony from Tanganyika and the South at Namanga and passing through Nairobi, Nakuru and Kisumu to cross the border into Uganda at Busia. The Nairobi-Nakuru section of this road (approximately 100 miles in length) is bitumenised and work is at present in progress on bitumenising the seventeen miles between Nairobi and Athi River. Sixteen miles of bitumen road to the west of Nakuru are practically finished and work has started on the construction of the road westward towards Londiani.
Work has also started on the construction of a road on a new alignment leading northward from Thika to Sagana.
The recently realigned Nairobi-Kiambu road is scheduled for bitumen surfacing this year (1951).

Work on the realignment of the road from Mombasa to Mackinnon Road (55 miles) continues and construction of the bridges carrying the new road over the Maji-ya-Chumvi river and railway line is in progress. Apart from eight miles of bitumen surface near Mackinnon Road, the new road will not be bitumenised for the present.

TRADE AND CUSTOMS (See Note on page 27.)

In 1949 the Customs Departments of Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika were amalgamated under the title of the East African Customs and Excise Departments as an East Africa High Commission Service with Headquarters in Mombasa and Regional Offices in Mombasa, Kampala and Dar-es-Salaam.

Identical customs tariffs were enacted in Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika in 1923 and revised in 1930. Surcharges were introduced during the recent war and in 1946 these were incorporated in the Kenya Tariff. In 1948, however, the equivalent of the war-time surcharges were removed from the import duties on certain food stuffs, apparel, cotton piece goods, bicycles and parts and motor cars and parts.

Subsequent amendments to the tariff have been made with the intention of adjusting anomalies assisting reconstruction by removal of duties on certain builder's materials, and attempting to offset the high and rising cost of essential consumer goods such as shirts, underwear, paraffin, khaki drill and blankets by the removal or reduction of duties on these goods. The last mentioned alterations were made in 1950 by Kenya and Uganda, but similar alterations were not made in Tanganyika, and the tariffs of the three East African territories, while possessing a main common structure, now differ in minor respects.

The basic fiscal rate is 22 per cent. ad valorem assessed on the value which is taken as being the price which the goods would fetch on sale in the open market in the Colony if delivered at the port of importation, freight, insurance and all costs charges and expenses having been paid by the seller. Specific duties are charged on goods which lend themselves to such a form of assessment, the more important of such goods being potable spirits, cigarettes and tobacco, motor spirit tyres and tubes, matches and cement. In the case of certain articles such as piece goods wearing apparel and cycles, minimum specific duties have been fixed as an alternative to an ad valorem rate in order to safeguard the revenue against abnormally cheap supplies.

The free list includes the chief development imports such as machinery; road, rail, air and water transport equipment; coal, coke and fuel oil; packing materials; explosives; insecticides and agricultural and mining equipment; and builders' requisites and sanitaryware. Exemption from duty is also allowed in the case of drugs, medicines and disinfectants; shirts, vests, socks, stockings, pullovers, cardigans, jumpers; paraffin; filters and refrigerators, surgical and scientific instruments; educational requisites; religious articles; music and literature and bona fide baggage brought by passengers for personal and household use.

Provision is made in the tariff for the imposition of what are known as "suspended" duties on certain commodities which are produced and manufactured in one or more of the territories—Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika. These duties may be imposed, varied or revoked unilaterally thus allowing a modified control by each territory over the degree of protection afforded to a number of the local products.
By a tripartite Customs Agreement in 1927, a system of inter-territorial transfers of imported goods was introduced between Kenya, Uganda
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
and Tanganyika. The effect of this agreement is virtually to remove customs barriers between the three territories the duty being credited to the receiving territory by a transfer form procedure.
The Congo Basin Treaty obligations prevent the grant of Imperial Preference.

In 1950 the value for Kenya trade imports excluding imports on Government account, railway material and goods in transit and transhipment was £27,979,691. In the same period the value for exports (domestic exports and re-exports) was £20,737,006.

N.B.—Figures for Kenya are for the first time shown separately and do not include Uganda. In addition the figures for net imports do not conform to those of previous years since they are calculated on a different basis. They now represent goods imported for consumption or warehousing in Kenya, including goods which are subsequently re-exported outside East Africa. In addition the value of goods transferred during the year from one East African territory to another has been deducted from the figures for the year's imports of the territory from which they have come and added to those of the territory to which they have been sent, even though the goods in question may have been imported from abroad during a previous year. The figures of re-exports to foreign countries included in the Trade Exports figure for the year merely represent the figures of imported goods which were subsequently re-exported to places outside East Africa and do not include re-exports to another East African territory. Accordingly the Trade Exports figure for 1950 does not conform to those shown for preceding years.

In 1950 the main Net Imports into Kenya were:—Food products, beverages and Tobacco £2,327,168; Chemicals and allied products £1,505,982; Rubber and manufactures thereof £724,313; Textiles £3,310,395; Clothing of all materials and miscellaneous textile goods £2,098,525; Products for heating, lighting, power and lubricants £4,282,291; Non-metallic minerals and manufactures thereof £1,730,891; Base metals and manufactures thereof £4,635,195; Machinery, apparatus, appliances and vehicles £8,464,268.

The Principal Countries of Origin were:—United Kingdom £19,605,158; Bahrein Islands £1,905,267; India £2,744,246; Union of South Africa £1,428,638; Other parts of the British Commonwealth £655,070 (Total British Commonwealth £26,338,379); Italy £568,188; Japan £376,646; Persia £1,633,839; United States of America £1,606,761; Other Foreign Countries £3,554,051 (Total Foreign Countries £7,739,485).

The Principal Domestic Exports of Kenya during 1950 were:—Coffee £3,559,553; Tea £1,333,854; Other food products, beverages and tobacco £1,905,439; Fatty substances and waxes, animal and vegetable £149,828; Sodium carbonate £860,511; Wattle bark extract £978,945; Hides, Skins, leather and manufactures thereof £1,978,973; Cotton lint £227,823; Pyrethrum £330,723; Sisal £4,061,168; Gold—unworked £355,979.
The Chief Countries of Destination were:—United Kingdom £6,056,845 (Total British Commonwealth £10,437,649); United States of America £2,267,109; (Total Foreign Countries £6,621,528).
The net Customs Revenue for 1950 was £4,008,017.

THE LEEWARD ISLANDS
See pages 222–234 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
EDUCATION
During the year 1950 the cost of education in the Colony was $565,359.
CIVIL SERVICE
A cost-of-living allowance is now paid on salaries as follows :—
50 per cent. on the first £100. 30 per cent. on the second £100. 20 per cent. on the third £100.
COMMUNICATIONS
Shipping

Communications between the Colony and the United Kingdom are maintained by vessels of the Harrison Line which call approximately once a month at Antigua and St. Kitts to discharge cargo and load sugar and cotton for the United Kingdom. During the crop season for sugar more frequent calls are made. These vessels call about four times during the last five months of each year at Montserrat in order to load cotton for the United Kingdom. In 1950, a vessel belonging to the Compagnie Générale Transatlantique also maintained a regular service between Antigua and the United Kingdom.
Irregular inter-island services are provided by schooners, sloops and two converted M.L.Cs.
Air

British West Indian Airways maintain a thrice-weekly service between Antigua and St. Kitts, and regular services from Antigua to other parts of the British West Indies. The service to Jamaica connects with the British Overseas Airways Corporation's service to the United Kingdom.

Pan-American World Airways maintain a service between Puerto Rico and Trinidad, which calls thrice weekly northbound and southbound at Antigua and at St. Thomas in the U.S. Virgin Islands whence Tortola in the British Virgin Islands may easily be reached by motor launch.
K.L.M. maintain a service between Curacao and St. Martin, which calls at Guadeloupe and St. Kitts en route.

CURRENCY AND BANKING

Following unification of the currency note system in the Eastern Group of British Caribbean Territories (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and the Windward Islands) in 1951, the currency includes notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board. Legislative provision exists for the eventual issue of Eastern Caribbean coinage but, meanwhile, British silver continues in currency.

FEDERATION OF MALAYA
See pages 234–255 of The Colonial Office List 1951.

(These notes were prepared by the Federal Government in mid-1951 and consequently do not refer to Sir Henry Gurney's death and to the recent developments. It is intended to include in the 1953 edition a more comprehensive review than has been possible in this first restricted edition.

Further information may be obtained from the Annual Report on the Federation of Malaya 1951 which will be published by the Government Press, Kuala Lumpur and, subsequently, by H.M. Stationery Office, London. The Annual Report for 1950 is at present obtainable from H.M. Stationery Office).
SOCIAL SERVICES
Health
During 1950, 3,892,000 patients were treated either as out-patients or in-patients in the fixed and travelling dispensaries and welfare clinics in the rural areas.
Labour

No serious strikes occurred during the period under review, and although there were a number of industrial disputes and some protracted negotiations, industrial peace was generally maintained. This is significant when viewed against its proper background, as many wage adjustments took place as the selling price of tin and rubber increased to no small degree during this period. Unfortunately the cost of living has steadily risen so that the improvement in real wages has not been as substantial as a cursory glance at wage increases would indicate.

There was, during 1950, a distinct movement of labour away from the larger plantations to the smaller holdings, where very high wages (but few amenities) obtained, owing to the operation of various sharing systems and as a direct result of slack supervision caused by the absence of many managers and owners. However, towards the end of 1950 and the beginning of 1951, this movement slackened as the smallholdings approached saturation point and as workers began also to appreciate the value of amenities and the better chance of security on the larger plantations.

A total of 37,067 man-days were lost during 1950, compared with 5,390½ man-days in 1949. The increase in 1950 was mainly due to demands for wage increases and to differences over the interpretation of certain wage-rate directives in the rubber industry. Mine workers were, on the whole, satisfied with a bonus scheme which increased their earnings as the price of tin improved. After two agreed wage increases had been given in the rubber industry during 1950, further negotiations failed, and the parties decided to go to arbitration. A Board of Arbitration was convened in January, 1951.

The figures for the production of tin and rubber during 1950 and the early part of 1951 are in themselves a remarkable tribute to the employers and workers in the Federation, who have been living under the dangerous and insecure conditions of a serious emergency. The "Briggs" plan for regrouping of labour forces has meant that many thousands of workers have had to move their homes and this has thrown a tremendous strain on them and also on their employers. The steadiness and industry of the established labour forces in the larger places of employment have been of inestimable value to the Malayan economy.
Welfare
The technical services of the Department of Social Welfare have been strengthened. Juvenile Courts and an effective Probation Service are in being throughout the Federation of Malaya.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

As from 1st January, 1951, the functions under the heading of "General Welfare" became the responsibility of the State and Settlement Governments. Social Welfare Officers and their staffs concerned with those functions are now an integral part of the State and Settlement Establishments.

The departmental training schemes have been continued and extended with success. The training course for Malayan Probation Officers held in Malacca in 1950 enabled the setting up of the Juvenile Courts and Probation Services.

The Central Welfare Council and the State and Settlement Welfare Committees, now independent bodies, together with other voluntary organisations, are taking an increasing part in personal social service and in dealing with special problems as in the campaign against tuberculosis and the building up of blind welfare services. A Malayan Youth Council has been set up. Youth Services have expanded. There has been an increase in the number of rural community centres and village halls.
Information Services

Since the war, a Department of Information has been established. The fundamental purpose of the Information machinery of Government may be defined as being to provide a sustained and intensive effort, with a planned strategy, to raise the level of public understanding and to inculcate in the people interest in, and knowledge of, their political, social and economic responsibilities.
In 1951 community radios were installed in some 744 Malayan villages.
The Malayan Film Unit has been permanently established and its locally-trained staff are now producing work of considerable value.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads
During 1950, 467 miles of road were re-metalled, hard surface or bitumen sealed and the programme of gradual replacement of old and unserviceable bridges and culverts was continued.
The expenditure in 1950 on the maintenance of the 5,373 miles of metalled roads in the Federation amounted to $17,679,000, giving an average cost of $3,290 per mile.

MALTA
See pages 255–263 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
GENERAL DESCRIPTION
In December, 1950 there were 72 vessels registered at Malta, the gross tonnage being 19,838.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
In 1950 there were 111 primary schools with 43,613 pupils.
The number of literacy classes fell to 82 during the year, the attendance being 1,200.
Public Works

The following smaller towns and villages have an efficient system of sewage disposal : Cottonera, Sliema, St. Julians, Msida, Pieta, Kalkara, Zabbar, Hamrun, Qormi, Balzan, Lija, Attard, Birkirkara, Mdina, Rabat, Tarxien, Pawla, Zejtun, Ghaxaq and Luqa.

During the financial year 1950–51, interceptors from Zebbug to Siggiewi, and Ghaxaq to Gudja were continued, and delivery mains started from the proposed pumping stations at Birzebbuga and Dingli to Ghaxaq and Rabat respectively.

Street sewers have been laid in the villages of Mellieha, Zebbug, Mosta, Naxxar, and new sections in Hamrun and Birkirkara. Small extensions have been carried out in Qormi, Rabat, Pawla, Tarxien, Zabbar, Gzira, Pieta and St. Julians.

With regard to the general scheme for the collection and disposal of domestic refuse, two consignments of the necessary machinery were delivered during 1950 and the delivery of the remaining consignment is expected by the end of 1951.
Xaghra should be added to the list of villages in Gozo supplied with electric current.
COMMUNICATIONS
There are now 4,724 telephone exchange lines with 7,231 stations.

MALTA
FINANCE (See Note on page 27.)
In 1950–51 the total Revenue amounted to £5,720,191 and the Expenditure to £5,802,106. Grants and loans under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts, received during the year, amounted to £76,901.
BANKING AND CURRENCY
The amount of local currency in circulation on the 31st March, 1951, was £15,497,882 10s. 0d.
Barclays Bank (D.C. & O.) now has six branches in Malta.
On the 31st March, 1951, deposits in the Government Savings Bank amounted to £17,228,286, with 54,113 depositors.
TRADE Customs Tariff
The amount of duty collected in 1950 from specific duties was £1,274,559, and from ad valorem duties £1,081,805.
IMPORTS (See Note on page 27.)

The total value of imports in 1950 was £16,687,132, of which imports from the United Kingdom were valued at £7,032,661, from other British countries £3,167,951, and from elsewhere £6,486,520. In addition the value of bullion and specie imported amounted to £72,700.
EXPORTS (including Re-exports) (See Note on page 27.)

In 1950, total exports were valued at £1,660,759. Exports to the United Kingdom were valued at £675,867, to other British countries £122,952, and to elsewhere £861,940. The export of bullion and specie was valued at £450.
The value of re-exports to the United Kingdom in 1950 was £284,806; to other parts of the British Empire £43,689; to foreign countries £163,901; and as ships' stores, etc., £349,944.
SHIPPING ENTERED AND CLEARED
In 1950 the total tonnage of shipping entered was 1,985,617, and cleared 1,987,194, of which British tonnage amounted to 928,161, and 929,738 respectively.

NIGERIA
See pages 275–302 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
CONSTITUTION

A new Constitution for Nigeria was approved on the 29th June, 1951. The Territory is divided into three Regions, the Northern, Eastern and Western Regions, with Lagos and the Colony included in the Western Region. The Territory of the Cameroons is administered as an integral part of Nigeria in accordance with a Trusteeship Agreement dated 14th December, 1946.

The provisions of the new Constitution are recorded in the Nigeria (Constitution) Order in Council, 1951, and in the Nigeria (Supplementary Provisions) Order in Council, 1951. New Letters Patent and Royal Instructions were also issued during the year. The Supplementary Provisions Order in Council creates three offices of Lieutenant-Governor, one for each Region, replacing the previous offices of Chief Commissioner of the Northern, Eastern and Western Provinces.

The Constitution now provides for a Legislature in each Region and for a Central Legislature for the whole Territory. In the Northern and Western Regions the Regional Legislature consists of a House of Chiefs and a House of Assembly, and in the Eastern Region there is a single House, the House of Assembly. The Northern House of Chiefs consists of the Lieutenant-Governor as President, three official members, 50 Chiefs, and an Adviser on Moslem Law. The Northern House of Assembly consists of a President, 4 official members, 90 elected members, and special members up to a maximum of 10. The Western House of Chiefs consists of the Lieutenant-Governor as President, 3 official members, and 50 Chiefs. The Western House of Assembly consists of a President, 4 official members, 80 elected members and special members up to a maximum of 3. The Eastern House of Assembly consists of the Lieutenant-Governor as President, 5 official members, 80 elected members and special members up to a maximum of 3.

The Central Legislature consists of a House of Representatives of 148 members. Of these members 68 are elected by the two Houses of the Northern Legislature, 34 by the Western Legislature, and 34 by the Eastern House of Assembly. There are also 6 members to represent interests and communities not otherwise adequately represented, and 6 official members, being the Chief Secretary, the three Lieutenant-Governors, the Attorney-General, and the Financial Secretary.

In each Region there is a Regional Executive Council of which the Lieutenant-Governor is President. Each of these Councils contains also 3 ex-officio members and between 6 and 9 Ministers (between 7 and 9 in the case of the Eastern Region) selected from amongst members of the Legislatures.

At the Centre there is a Council of Ministers consisting of the Governor as President, the 6 official members of the House of Representatives, and 12 Ministers selected from amongst members of the House of Representatives in such a way that each of the three Regions shall be represented by 4 Ministers.

The Regional Legislatures have legislative jurisdiction over a specified range of subjects, of which the most important are Agriculture, Animal Health, Fisheries, Forestry, Local Industries, Co-operative Societies, Social Welfare, Education,
Digitized by Google
NIGERIA

Regional Public Works, Town and Country Planning, Customary Land Tenure and certain other Land matters. The Central Legislature has legislative jurisdiction on all subjects including those listed for Regional jurisdiction.

The principal function of the Executive Councils in the Regions and of the Council of Ministers at the Centre is to be the principal instrument of policy in respect of matters to which the jurisdiction of the Legislature extends.

Elected members of the Regional Houses of Assembly are elected by a process of indirect election. The stages in this process vary in each of the three Regions. Voters must be persons of the age of 21 years or upwards, being British subjects or protected persons, and either resident in, or natives of, the area in which they wish to vote. They must also either have paid tax in Nigeria or have been exempted from payment. In the Northern Region there is a further restriction of the franchise to males.
In the exercise of the prerogative power of pardon the Governor is advised by a small Privy Council composed of persons specially appointed for this purpose.
ADMINISTRATION

Nigeria is divided for administrative purposes into the Northern, Western and Eastern Regions. The Northern and Eastern Regions are identical with the former Northern and Eastern Provinces, but the Western Region includes the Colony as well as the former Western Provinces.
The Northern Region consists of the provinces of Adamawa, Bauchi, Benue, Bornu, Ilorin, Kabba, Kano, Katsina, Niger, Plateau, Sokoto and Zaria.
The Western Region consists of the Colony, and the provinces of Abeokuta, Benin, Ibadan, Ijebu, Oyo, Ondo and Warri.
The Eastern Region consists of the provinces of Calabar, Cameroons, Bamenda, Ogoja, Onitsha, Owerri and Rivers.
The provinces are each partitioned into divisions.

The seat of Central Government and place of residence of the Governor is Lagos. Each of the Regions is administered by a Lieutenant-Governor responsible to the Governor, the seats of administration being Kaduna (Northern Region), Enugu (Eastern Region), and Ibadan (Western Region). The Cameroons and Bamenda provinces are
administered by the Commissioner of the Cameroons, who is responsible to the Lieutenant-Governor, Eastern Region.

Each province is under the control of a Resident, assisted by a staff of district officers and assistant district officers who have charge of the various divisions into which the province is divided. The Residents and their assistants are responsible for the detailed administration of the country.

Native Authorities —In the early days of administration in the Northern Provinces the system known as "indirect rule" was set up. The effect of this system is that today the functions of local government are for the most part carried out through native chiefs and councils with the assistance of the British administrative staff. The native authorities are responsible, through the administrative staff, to the Government for the peace and good order of their respective areas. Certain essential services have been undertaken by the native authorities and maintained out of the revenue obtained from a share of the taxes collected by them, together with the whole of the native court receipts and various minor fees. The revenue of each native treasury is shown in annual estimates, together with the expenditure for the year, drawn up with the advice of the administrative staff and approved by the Lieutenant-Governor of the region.

Revenue and Expenditure

As will be seen from the figures given below the cost and extent of public services has risen greatly since before the 1939-1945 War. The increases have been met by raising the rates of direct and indirect tax levied upon a considerably greater volume of imports and exports and higher personal incomes.

In 1938-39 Revenue was some £6 million and Expenditure (omitting earnings and expenditure of the Nigerian Railway) some £7 million. Including Colonial Development and Welfare Schemes, financed by the U.K. Government, but excluding Nigerian Railway figures, Revenue and Expenditure totals for recent years are as follows :—
The General Revenue Balance (surplus) at the 31st March, 1951 amounted to £13,723,595. In addition, £6,564,544 was standing to the credit of the Revenue Equalisation Fund at that date.
The chief sources of revenue are Import, Export and Excise Duties which, in 1950-51 provided £18 million and Direct Taxes which
provided £5 million, the balance being made up of such items as revenue from the Marine, Posts and Telegraphs, Electricity and other Government Departments, Mining Royalties, Fees and Licences.
The chief items of expenditure fall into two groups; expenditure on Services, Works and Departments, on the one hand, and expenditure
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

on Development and Welfare schemes on the other. Of the first group, nearly £10 millions of the expenditure for 1951/52 is attributable to the cost of Defence, the Police, Marine Works and Services, Pensions and Gratuities, Posts and Telegraphs, Public Works, Agriculture and Forestry, Education, Electrical Works and Services, and Medical and Veterinary Services.
Expenditure on the second group, Development and Welfare, is financed jointly from Nigerian Revenue and Loans and from Colonial Development and Welfare Grants.

During 1951/52 more than £4 millions was provided in grants, and a further £1.6 millions was found from revenue. Schemes for improvement and advance in Agriculture, Industry, Education, Medical Services, Leprosy Control, and Water Supplies were among those planned for 1951/52, together with assistance for a number of research projects.

Direct Taxation
Two forms of direct taxation are in force, income tax and general tax.

Early in 1948 an arrangement was made with H.M. Government for the avoidance of double taxation, with retrospective effect from April, 1946. The main result of this arrangement, which is in line with similar arrangements made by H.M. Government with other countries including the U.S.A., Canada, Australia and a number of Colonies, is that the profits of a British trading concern having a permanent establishment in Nigeria bear the full rate of Nigerian Income Tax, the burden of relieving the resultant double taxation being borne by the U.K. Similar arrangements were made in 1950 with the Governments of the Gold Coast, Sierra Leone and the Gambia, with effect from April, 1950.

NIGERIA
CURRENCY AND BANKING

The unit of currency is the West African pound, maintained at par with sterling by the West African Currency Board which issues and redeems currency in the four British West African Colonies against deposits and withdrawals of sterling in London. The currency in issue comprises £1 and 10s. notes, and alloy and nickel-bronze coins of 2s., 1s., 6d., 3d., 1d., $\frac{1}{2}$d. and $\frac{1}{4}$d.

Banking facilities are provided in all the important towns by the Bank of British West Africa Limited, Barclays Bank (D.C. & O.), the Agbonmagbe Bank Ltd., the National Bank of Nigeria Ltd., the Nigerian Farmers Commercial Bank Ltd., the African Continental Bank, the Standard Bank of Nigeria Ltd., Pan Nigeria Bank and the British and French Bank (for Commerce and Industry) Ltd.
PRODUCTION AND TRADE
(See Note on page 27.)

Secondary Industries. The largest factories in the country are the African Plywood and Timber Co. at Sapele; the factory of the British-American Tobacco Co. at Ibadan which produces 700 million cigarettes a year; and a soap factory and a brewery both at Apapa. Other industries of importance are :—
(i) Groundnut Mills : There are three groundnut mills operating in Kano under private enterprise with a total throughput of some 20,000 tons of groundnuts per year.
(ii) Palm Oil Mills : Each with a throughput of 1,000 to 2,000 tons of fruit per annum. 9,250 tons of fruit were handled during 1950/51 representing sales of oil and kernels to the value to £90,000.

(iii) Textiles : The development of the traditional crafts of spinning, weaving and dyeing has been undertaken at a number of Textile Training Centres. Two power-operated weaving mills each with 60 looms have been erected at Lagos and Kano.
(iv) Pottery : A large-scale modern factory for production of domestic pottery is in process of erection near Lagos.

POPULATION
The latest population estimates, at the 6th May, 1951, are as follows :—
Europeans —37,220.

Africans
Female —835,495.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

European. At the end of 1950 there were 22 Government schools in the territory. Of these, seven offered primary education up to Standard 5 and secondary education up to Form III. Two others, while offering the full primary range, continued to Form II only. Eight schools provided instruction only within the primary range, four from K.G. to Standard 5 and four from K.G. to Standard 2. There were five Infants' Schools. The total enrolment at Government schools was 4,748.
Digitized by Google
NORTHERN RHODESIA

Certain controlled schools under private management catered for children below the permissive school age and others offered instruction within the Government school range. In addition the Lusaka Dominican Convent High School offered secondary education up to Cambridge School Certificate standard (Form IV). The total enrolment at controlled schools under private management was 1,141 including 229 children below the permissive school age.
Boarding accommodation was available at four Government schools and four private schools. In addition there were, in connection with Government schools, three aided private hostels.

By arrangement with the Southern Rhodesia Education Department correspondence tuition was provided as required for 61 children (mainly six to eight years old) living more than three miles from the nearest day school.
Coloured and Asiatic. Ketapola School, under Government management, offered education for coloured children in the primary range. There were four private government-aided Indian schools.
African. In 1950 there were 1,273 schools receiving grants-in-aid including 96 native authority schools. There are 50 Government schools.

The estimated expenditure on African Education for 1951 is £498,974, of which £252,150 is grants-in-aid for recurrent expenditure. In addition, the sum of £159,807 is estimated to be spent under the Colonial Development and Welfare Scheme.
Information Services
With regard to wireless programmes, the small-scale rediffusion experiment which was initiated in the main African area in Lusaka has proved very successful.

Owing to an improvement in the housing position it was possible to fill some vacancies in the Broadcasting (Engineers) Section of the Information Department and with the delivery of equipment the development of the transmission side of broadcasting has shown very satisfactory progress.
The 15 kw. transmitter has been installed.
Plans for extensions to studios and transmitters which will allow simultaneous broadcasting in several languages have been implemented.
Now that mobile recording equipment has arrived programmes of African plays and music are being expanded and new and outstanding material is being provided.
With regard to the development of the tourist industry, for which the Information Department is also responsible, a big-game safari scheme for a limited number of hunters is now in operation.
Labour

The number of persons in employment in mines, sawmills, railways and the Public Works Department in the territory in 1950 was 6,250 Europeans and 57,300 Africans. A further 8,000 Europeans and 125,000 Africans (estimated) were engaged in other forms of employment in the territory.

In the principal industry in the country (copper mining) the average monthly wages, exclusive of bonuses, cost-of-living allowance and overtime, etc., for Europeans were £48.48 (surface) and £54.02 (underground) and for Africans 62s. 2d. (surface) and 75s. 11d. (underground). To these earnings, which represent little more than half of the total remuneration, must be added the value
of sub-economic rents of housing etc., enjoyed by Europeans and free housing and food provided for Africans.
Wage rates in other industries and occupations continued to rise during 1950 but so did the cost of living. The index stood at 156 at the end of the year (1939 = 100).

The year brought two major disputes between the African Mineworkers' Trade Union and the four Copper Mining Companies. The first, relating to wage increases, was settled but the second, relating to the question of a profit-sharing scheme for African employees, was unresolved at the end of the year.

Disputes applying to the whole Territory occurred between the Northern Rhodesia African Drivers' Trade Union and the Associated Chambers of Commerce and Industry, and between the Northern Rhodesia African Shop Assistants' Trade Union and the same body during the year. Both were settled.

During the year there were three strikes by European workers and 71 by African workers. In the case of the latter the strikes were almost all of short duration, and were resolved on the spot by local Labour Officers. The total man-days lost by African workers amounted to 6,124 in strikes involving 4,777 workers. 1,732 Europeans lost 2,522 man-days.

The dispute inherited from 1949 between the Mine Workers' Union, supported by the African Mineworkers' Trade Union and the four Copper Mining Companies, over shorter working hours in the copper mining industry, was submitted to a Board of Inquiry which sat at Kitwe in September. The Board's report was published later in the year, and recommended that the normal weekly working hours should not be reduced to 40, but that they should not exceed 48, which they do at present, in some cases. The Board also recommended an extra annual paid holiday, and extended travelling time for European employees proceeding on leave. The Board also advised against the introduction of compulsory arbitration for disputes in the mining industry following upon the failure of conciliation.

Trade Unionism continued to spread amongst African workers, and by the end of the year there were seven Trade Unions. The number of European employee representative bodies remained at four, with a like number of Associations representing employers.

As a result of negotiations between the Northern Rhodesia Master Builders' and Allied Trades Association and the Northern Rhodesia General Workers' Trade Union, a very comprehensive agreement covering wages and conditions of employment was reached. This agreement lays down, for the first time in the building industry, uniform rates of wages, a 48-hour week, payment rate of time and a half for overtime, three paid Public Holidays per year, and provision for paid annual leave. The Agreement also provides for the payment to all employees of an inclusive wage in place of the old arrangement of cash wage plus food ration. The inclusive wage now includes a cash equivalent of rations and, in order to assist labourers in the new idea of feeding themselves, this cash equivalent is paid weekly. The balance of wages is paid at the end of the month or the ticket, as the case may be. This experiment appears to be working very well, and it also seems to have some effect in combating absenteeism.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
A further big step forward in industrial relations has been taken by both sides in the building industry in the setting up of a Joint Industrial Council.

One of the most important Ordinances affecting both African and European labour introduced during the year was the Silicosis Ordinance (No. 27 of 1950), which was brought into operation on 1st November, 1950.

On 10th July, 1950, the Apprenticeship (Amendment) Ordinance (No. 6 of 1950) was enacted. This was designed to prevent articles and certificates of apprenticeship being given except under the Ordinance.

The scope of the Apprenticeship Ordinance was considerably widened on 3rd October, 1950, by the schedule of trades being made applicable to all industries and undertakings, including the Government of Northern Rhodesia.

The Employment of Natives (Amendment) Ordinance (No. 9 of 1950) enacted on 21st July, 1950, provided that an African employee might, with the approval of the Commissioner for Labour and Mines and with his own consent, be charged rent for housing provided for him by his employer. This amendment was introduced to meet the wishes of Africans for an inclusive wage system and approval was given in the first instance only in respect of the higher grades of mine employees.

The Employment of Women, Young Persons and Children (Amendment) Ordinance (No. 49 of 1950) was introduced to carry out more effectively the intention of Ordinance No. 45 of 1949, to prevent the employment of young persons between the ages of twelve and sixteen in industrial undertakings without permission. This Ordinance had not come into operation at the end of the year.

Under the provisions of the Trade Unions and Trade Disputes Ordinance, 1949, the Trade Unions (Registration) Regulations, 1950, were made by the Governor in Council on 13th May, 1950. The Regulations prescribed the manner of application for registration of a Trade Union, the forms to be used, the making of annual returns and the fees payable.

The Employment of Natives Regulations were amended on 4th July, 1950, to permit the Director of Medical Services and the Commissioner for Labour and Mines to approve, subject to certain safeguards, temporary buildings to be used for a period of not more than ten years, provided that each year after the second year a Labour Officer and a Medical Officer jointly certified that these buildings were fit for human habitation.

The staff of the Labour Branch of the Department, excluding officers engaged purely on clerical duties, was 20, in the Trade Testing branch three officers, in the Apprenticeship branch one officer; the Workmen's Compensation Commissioner, who was also a member of the Department, performed the duties of Controller of Apprenticeship in addition to his other duties.

For the purpose of the Workmen's Compensation Ordinance a workman is now, with certain exceptions, an employee whose earnings do not exceed £1,200 per annum (as compared with £950 per annum previously).
Welfare
Government employs three women Welfare Officers and one male Welfare Officer, although no department of Social Welfare has yet been formed.

The Salvation Army operates a Home of Refuge for European children and an Old Men's Home. Both Homes are at Ndola. An additional children's home was opened during 1951 and is operated by the Diocese of Northern Rhodesia. This home is also at Ndola.

African welfare in urban areas is very largely the concern of local Government authorities and of large employers of labour, who employ both European and African Welfare Officers. The duties of these officers include the organisation of sport and general recreational activities, youth clubs, handicrafts, libraries, etc.
A number of patriotic and charitable bodies exist. All appeals for charitable purposes are conducted by the Central Charities Committee, a non-official body set up for the purpose.
Compassionate and old age allowances are payable to Europeans who fulfil the necessary conditions.
THE RHODES-LIVINGSTONE INSTITUTE

The Rhodes-Livingstone Institute was founded in 1937 by Sir Hubert Young to undertake sociological research in Central Africa. Its funds are drawn partly from private donations and partly from a Government grant and it has also received a grant from Colonial Development and Welfare funds. During 1950, members of the Institute were studying the Lunda, Yao, Shona and Ndebele peoples, besides problems of African urban life and the economic life of the Tonga people. The Institute also publishes a number of occasional papers on anthropology and related subjects.
COMMUNICATIONS
Aviation
The Air Pilot of Northern Rhodesia (1950 Edition) together with the Air Law (1951 Edition) provide detailed information on aviation in Northern Rhodesia.

One International and three National Airports lying along the North/South corridor route throughout the territory provide Customs, Health and Immigration controls as well as fuel, Air Traffic Control, radio and radio navigation aids. In all, 42 airfields are maintained, of which 14 are constructed for use in all weathers.
Central African Airways provide both regional and internal scheduled services with a fleet of aircraft consisting of 7 Vikings, 5 Doves and 4 Beavers.
They operate routes as follows :—
(a) Internal
(i) Lusaka, Fort Jameson—Thrice weekly each way (with additional weekly excursion to Monkey Bay).
(ii) Lusaka, Livingstone—Twice weekly each way.
(iii) Lusaka, Mankoya, Mongu, Kalabo, Balovale, Livingstone—Twice weekly.
(iv) Lusaka, Broken Hill*, Ndola, Kasama, Abercorn—Five times weekly each way.
* Flagstop.

(b) Regional Services
(i) Johannesburg, Salisbury, Lusaka, Ndola, Kasama, Abercorn, Tabara, Nairobi.
(ii) Johannesburg, Livingstone, Lusaka, Ndola—Twice weekly each way.
(iii) Johannesburg, Blantyre, Dar-es-Salaam, Nairobi—Twice weekly.
(iv) Fort Jameson, Lilongwi, Blantyre—Twice weekly.
East African Airways connect Fort Jameson with Nairobi, Salisbury and Blantyre twice weekly.
Zambesi Airways is, at present, the only Charter operator in the Territory and is based at Livingstone, using Rapides, Fox Moths and Fairchild aircraft.
The Northern Rhodesia Flying Club retains its main activities on the copperbelt but with zonal branches at Lusaka and Livingstone.
Posts, Telegraphs and Wireless
The daily mail service by rail between post offices on the line of rail is now supplemented by air mails between certain main centres.
The South African Airways (Springbok Service) handling airmails for overseas countries now operates three times a week.

There are now 66 post offices (including one mobile post office), 15 of which are money order offices and 56 telegraph offices. The main telegraph and trunk route lies alongside the railway line from the Victoria Falls to Chingola with branches from Kitwe to Mufulira, and from Ndola to Luanshya and the Belgian Congo Border. There is also a radio telephone link between Lusaka and Fort Jameson.

Wireless stations at Abercorn, Balovale, Broken Hill, Chinsali, Feira, Fort Jameson, Fort Rosebery, Isoka, Kasama, Kasempa, Kawambwa, Livingstone, Lundazi, Luwingu, Mankoya, Mkoshi, Mongu, Mpika, Mporokoso, Mumbwa, Mwinilunga, Nakonde, Namwala, Ndola, Petauke, Ridgeway (Lusaka), Senanga, Serenje, Sesheke, Solwezi and Shiwangandu maintain a public service.

TRADE AND INDUSTRY
Agriculture

The total cattle population in 1950 was 907,000, an increase of $4\frac{1}{2}\%$ on the previous year. 136,000 were owned by Europeans and 771,000 by Africans. 600,000 of the African-owned cattle were in the Southern and Barotse Provinces.
Cattle prices were increased during the year, and 32,000 head were sold for slaughter. Supplies from Barotseland are increasing, and buying is now effected by the trade.

An outbreak of Foot and Mouth Disease was controlled by the inoculation of 40,000 head with a heat-attenuated live virus. 20,000 doses of phenanthridinium were issued for the control of trypanosomiasis, and 270,000 doses of anthrax vaccine were supplied.

The greater portion of the territory is undeveloped agriculturally. The indigenous population is self-supporting in its food supply, nearly the whole of its requirements being produced in the village gardens. There is little general trade in food commodities except in the railway belt where the surplus native maize crop is marketed by the Maize Control Board. This surplus in 1950 amounted to 359,000 bags. The staple native cereals are finger millet ( Eleusine coracana ), Kaffir corn ( Sorghum ) and maize. The millet is produced over extensive tracts of the territory on a system of shifting cultivation whereby grain crops are grown in rotation with forest re-growth. Other native crops are bullrush millet ( Pennisetum typhoideum ), groundnuts, cassava, sweet potatoes and a variety of curcurbits and legumes.
Tobacco is widely cultivated by the natives but mainly on a small scale for private consumption, except in the Petauke District of the Eastern
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Province where there is a flourishing Burley tobacco industry with a production of 327,000 lb. in 1950. The industry is now organised on co-operative lines.

In the tracts adjoining the railway line considerable areas are given over to farming and ranching operations under European management. In the Fort Jameson district there is also a large area taken up by European farmers mainly engaged in tobacco growing.

Maize and tobacco are the chief crops of the settler. In the season 1949/50 the total maize on the line of rail was 571,000 bags. The season was favourable for maize growing and there was also an increase in the acreage planted over the previous season.
Wheat growing under irrigation has almost disappeared, production in 1950 being only 3,200 bags. Farmers are finding potato, onion and vegetable growing more lucrative than wheat growing.
Tobacco growers enjoyed a favourable season. The Eastern area crop reached a figure of 3,214,500 lb., while in the Western area the production was 2,956,000 lb.
Turkish tobacco production in the Western tobacco area continued at the low level of the previous year, only 100,000 lb. being produced in 1950.
The Headquarters of the Department of Agriculture are at Lusaka.
Forestry
The declared Forest Reserves and Protected Forest Areas now total 2,155 sq. miles.

The mining companies cut in 1950 a further 1,281,200 cubic feet of local timber and another 580,000 cubic feet was cut in the territory for minor industries. Other recorded consumption in 1950 included 371,800 poles and 524,000 cords of firewood.

The staff of the Forestry Branch of the Department of Agriculture had increased to 16 professional, 21 technical and 5 clerical European officers and a permanent staff of 255 Africans besides casual labour by the end of 1950.
Land Settlement

Crown land is now granted only on lease, normally for terms of 999 years in the case of farms and 99 years in the case of plots in townships and residential smallholdings, although in special circumstances, leases are granted for shorter periods. As regards agricultural land, preliminary leases are granted in the first instance, the term being 2 years for farms and 3 years for agricultural smallholdings (i.e. areas up to 300 acres). Personal occupation is required by lessees of farms. If the lessee develops his land to the satisfaction of the Crown, on the expiration of his preliminary lease he is granted a lease for 999 years.

Crown land suitable for mixed farming, ranching and tobacco-growing is available within easy reach of the railway, though good mixed farming land is now becoming scarce. All farms are unimproved and valuations range from 6s. 8d. to 30s. per acre. Some privately-owned freehold farms are in the market at prices ranging from £2 to £10 or more per acre according to the degree of development. A settler must be in possession of at least £3,000 capital actually available for farming purposes in order to obtain a grant of a

leasehold farm from Government. In approved cases, however, New Settlement Loans of up to £1,500 are granted by the Land Board. Applications for Crown land in Northern Rhodesia should be addressed to the Deputy Director of Surveys and Lands, Livingstone.
Minerals

The Department of Mines administers the Mining Ordinance, the Mining and Explosives Regulations, the Mining (Silicosis Prevention) Regulations, the Winding Engine Driver's (Certificate of Competency) Regulations, the Gold Trade Ordinance and the Factories Ordinance and Factories (Safety) Regulations. The Registration of mining titles is carried out by the Registrar of Titles in the Department of Surveys and Lands.

The copper produced in the territory in 1950 totalled 276,433 tons, valued at £43,410,364 against 259,081 tons valued at £31,255,168 in 1949. The production of the various copper mines in 1950 was:—Mufulira, 81,317 tons of blister copper; Nchanga, 34,545 tons of blister copper, 10,529 tons of electrolytic copper, and 259 tons of copper concentrates which were sold as concentrates; Nkana, 11,938 tons of blister copper, 67,144 tons of electrolytic copper and 30 tons of copper (other); Roan Antelope, 70,957 tons of blister copper. As a by-product Nkana also produced 34,914 cwtS. of cobalt alloy and 1,296 ozs. of gold, 173,304 ozs. of silver and 27,056 lbs. of selenium, which were recovered from refinery slimes.
Lead, zinc and vanadium are produced at Broken Hill, but no vanadium was recovered during 1950.

Some of the lead is fabricated at the mine into pipes and sheets for use in the metallurgical plants at Broken Hill and at the copper mines. No iron ore was mined during 1950. Iron ore and manganese ore are used as reagents in the production of lead and zinc. The value of the major products from Broken Hill during 1950 was: lead, £1,537,559, zinc, £3,126,650.
Other mineral activities are on a small scale.
