THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
NYASALAND PROTECTORATE
See pages 322–330 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
GENERAL DESCRIPTION
The second sentence of the first paragraph under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 , beginning "The south-eastern boundary ..." should be disregarded.
The provinces of the Protectorate are subdivided into 17 administrative areas, each in charge of a district commissioner, who is also a district magistrate.
POPULATION
The estimated population at the end of 1950 was Europeans, 3,820 ; Asiatics, 5,248 ; Africans, 2,340,126.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

The Five-Year Educational Plan for the years 1950–54 is now in operation. The first Five-Year plan for 1945–49 aimed at laying the foundations of a ten-year expansion programme. During this period half the recurrent expenditure was met by the Colonial Development and Welfare Fund, whereas the whole recurrent cost of the second five years will be borne locally. As a result of the Educational Survey which was completed in 1950 and which covered all existing facilities for African primary education in every district, vital information has been acquired on a local basis of what is needed for the development and consolidation of the primary school system. An agreed expansion programme has been drawn up for each district detailing the development in each year from 1950–54.

The Governor is advised on education policy by four separate committees for African, European, Asian and Euroafrican education. Each committee contains representatives of the section of the community concerned. Each administrative district has a School Committee which advises the Director of Education on local matters. Policy is implemented under the supervision of the Education Department which inspects all schools, controls and maintains Government schools, conducts Government and public examinations and pays all local educational grants. The staff of the Department consists of 39 Europeans, 48 Africans, two Indians and two Euroafricans. The total expenditure on Education for all races in 1950 amounted to £180,150.
Information Services
The Public Relations branch of the Secretariat was constituted as a separate Government Department in 1951.
COMMUNICATIONS
Postal
Air mails from and to adjoining countries and overseas are received and despatched daily, except Tuesdays.
There are now 50 post offices and 12 postal agencies in the Protectorate.
Telegraphs
The African trans-continental telegraph line has in the past provided the main trunk telegraph route between Southern Rhodesia and Tanganyika, with Zomba and Blantyre as the principal stations

connected thereto. Telegraph facilities have, however, now been supplemented by the provision of radio teleprinter working between Zomba and Salisbury, and a hand speed radio link between Zomba-Lusaka and Zomba-Beira. Hand speed radio telegraph stations for internal communications have been provided at Zomba, Karonga, Likoma Island, Lilongwe, Mkhoma, Mzuzu, Kasungu, Mzimba, Njakwa, Fort Johnston and Salima.
Telephones
All towns in the Southern Province are connected by telephone and by the end of 1951 communication will also be established to Lilongwe and a number of points in the Central Province.

External telephone communications are provided for by means of a Zomba-Salisbury radio telephone link, which gives service to all parts of Southern Rhodesia, to certain parts of the Union of South Africa and to the port of Beira in Portuguese East Africa.

TRADE AND COMMERCE
(See Note on page 27.)

In 1950 tobacco exports reached the figure of 24 million lb. representing 46 per cent. of the total volume of the Protectorate's export trade; tea exports totalled 15 million lb. or 28 per cent., cotton lint, 3 million lb., and soya beans, 473,000 lb. In 1950 344 long tons of tung oil, valued at £46,000, were exported. Other exports included sisal fibre, rubber, cotton seed, and tea, tobacco and rubber shooks.

Principal imports were cotton manufactures, £1,498,000 (of which £1,299,000 represented cotton piece goods); iron, steel and metal manufactures, £309,000; petrol, £158,000; vehicles and parts, £880,000; wood and timber manufactures, £152,000; earthenware and chinaware, £128,000; agricultural machinery, £65,000; industrial machinery, £438,000; bicycles, £185,000; hardware and hollow-ware, £272,000; wearing apparel and haberdashery, £157,000; provisions (including salt and sugar), £423,000; oils and fats (soap making), £43,000; chemicals, drugs, dyes, etc., £237,000; leather manufactures (boots and shoes), £31,000; tyres and tubes, £84,000.
The value of the principal exports were:— Tobacco, £2,766,731; tea, £1,690,712; cotton lint, £335,073; tung oil, £46,431; soya beans, £6,385.

The U.K. supplied 47 per cent. of import merchandise, other British countries 27 per cent. and foreign countries 26 per cent. Of the latter, the contribution from the United States of America was 8 per cent. Of export produce, the U.K. absorbed 63 per cent., other British countries 19 per cent. and foreign countries 18 per cent.

ST. HELENA
See pages 330–334 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
CLIMATE
The rainfall figures in inches for 1950 are Plantation, 46.85 ; Hutts' Gate, 43.23 ; Briars, 27.48 ; and Jamestown, 8.29.
POPULATION
The estimated population of St. Helena in December, 1950 was 4,695. The death rate per 1,000 inhabitants in 1950 was 10.65 and the birth rate, 28.99.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

The amalgamation of the primary church schools and the Government schools has been successful. The total number of scholars in the Colony at the end of 1950 was 1,313, the secondary school being attended by 57 pupils.
Poor Relief
The revenue and expenditure of the Poor Relief Board for the year 1950 was £1,736 and £1,881 respectively.
COMMUNICATIONS

The Union Castle Mail Steamship Company's vessels call at the Island approximately twice a month—once in each direction on their "round Africa" service. Intervals between arrivals and departures of mails are variable.
The rate of postage for letters to foreign countries is now 4d. for the first ounce and 2½d. for each additional ounce.

CURRENCY AND BANKING
There is no local currency. The legal tender is United Kingdom currency; South African currency also circulates. The Government issues bills of exchange in London at par.
The amounts standing to credit of depositors in the Government Savings Bank on the 31st December, 1950, was £110,203.
INDUSTRY, TRADE AND CUSTOMS (See Note on page 27.)

The Government flax mill is leased to a local company. There are now seven other mills operating in the island, the amount of fibre and tow exported in 1950 being 1,200 and 643 tons respectively. The amount of rope and twine exported during the same year was 36 tons.
The export of lily bulbs to England has suffered a decline owing to the prevalence of disease, but it is hoped to revive it again to the benefit of the Colony, particularly to small growers.

The number of ships calling at Jamestown for supplies, including steamers and ships of war, was 27 in 1950. The tonnage of shipping entered and cleared during the year was 179,302, of which British tonnage amounted to 178,239.

In 1950 the total imports of the Colony were valued at £120,132, of which £74,968 represented imports from the United Kingdom. The total exports were valued at £140,212, of which £134,526 represented exports to the United Kingdom.

ST. HELENA : SARAWAK
ASCENSION
The population of Ascension in 1950 was 176, of whom 130 were St. Helenians.
TRISTAN DA CUNHA
In 1950, the islanders numbered 240, and there were 26 resident non-islanders. There were about 260 head of cattle on the island.

Except during the war years it had become the practice to arrange, if possible, for a ship to call annually to the Island with mails and supplies. With the establishment of the fishing industry in 1949, five or six calls a year during the summer months have, however, become the rule.

In 1950 an Island Council, which had been in informal existence for some 25 years, was reorganised on the basis of three non-islander members (including the Administrator as Chairman) and 17 Islander members (including six women representatives).

December to March is the period of the year that the weather is most likely to be settled and suitable for visiting and landing upon the Island. But in all seasons, except the mid-winter of June, July and August, weather conditions quite often permit the islanders to voyage in open canvas boats to Inaccessible and Nightingale Islands to gather birds and eggs, shear the sheep kept on Inaccessible, etc.

For flour, sugar, jam, and other prepared or manufactured food the Island is dependent upon imports. Apple trees are in fair number and bear well; there are also peaches, vines, a few fig trees and a plentiful supply of local "cranberry." The main tree of the island, Phylica nitida , which resembles a juniper and upon which the islanders still largely depend for fuel, has been cut out in the immediate vicinity of the settlement, but still covers at least 20% of the Island. Attempts are now being made to establish this, Insignis Pine, and other suitable species, on a plantation basis.

The meteorological and wireless station which is still maintained on the Island by the Government of South Africa is in contact with passing shipping daily from 12.00 to 13.00 hours G.M.T., for the purpose of giving and receiving weather reports on a frequency of 500 Kc/s.

Fish of good quality is plentiful. There are rich crayfish beds, and some 60 tons of tails were exported in 1950. The canning factory of the Tristan Development Company, the South African company referred to in The Colonial Office List 1951 , was completed at the end of 1950.

Notes.

Free quarters are provided for expatriate officers and for locally-appointed officers in the Staff Grade. The basis on which the value of free quarters is reckoned for pension purposes is a sum not exceeding one-sixth of the salary of the officer's appointment.
SARAWAK
See pages 334–342 of The Colonial Office List 1951 . SOCIAL SERVICES Information Services

The Government of Sarawak Information Service, which is a branch of the office of the Chief Secretary, was established in January, 1951, and has since that date been considerably expanded. G.S.I.S. conducts the Government's relations with the Sarawak and Malayan Press to which it supplies information and guidance on Government policy and activity, and co-ordinates official publicity campaigns. It produces two monthly newspapers in Malay and Iban and operates five mobile cinemas which give free shows to the public and to schools. It distributes pamphlets, newspapers, periodicals and posters in English, Malay and Chinese throughout the country, and has opened a public Information Reading Room in Kuching. It is hoped that more Reading Rooms will shortly be opened in other centres, which will be a base for community radio listening.
Welfare

A Social Welfare Council has also been established, which includes representatives of all the various organisations which are interested in this kind of work, as well as of the general public. The Council co-ordinates all existing voluntary social welfare activities in the Colony, sponsors such welfare projects as it approves of, and initiates new projects.
A Girls' Home was established at the end of 1950 and is being managed by the Salvation Army with financial assistance from the Government.
COMMUNICATIONS
Air
The new airport at Kuching, which possesses modern air radio and navigational aids, was officially opened to traffic in September, 1950.
Air communication with Singapore, Labuan, Jesselton and Sandakan is at present maintained by means of services four times weekly in each direction operated by Malayan Airways Dakota aircraft.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
A new runway is under construction at Sibu mainly financed by Colonial Development and Welfare funds.
Posts and Telegraphs
“Tatau” should be added to the list of Post Offices given under this sub-heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 .
The air-mail service with Singapore, Labuan, Jesselton and Sandakan, maintained by aircraft of Messrs. Malayan Airways Ltd., is now operated four times weekly.
With regard to telegraph rates to Commonwealth countries, the Government Ordinary full rate is now 25 cents per word.
832 telephones are now connected in local centres of which 404 are in use in Government offices and bungalows.
PUBLIC FINANCE (See Note on page 27.)

The Colony has no public debt. Approximately two-thirds of the revenue is derived from Customs import and export duties; other sources are excise duties, licences and taxes which include income tax on Companies and revenue from Government property. The only direct taxes levied on the native population are the Dayak “door tax” of $1 per family per year and the Malay “head tax” of $1 which is levied on all males of working age.
The main heads of expenditure are Pensions and Provident Fund, Public Works, Medical and Health Department, Constabulary and District Administration.

In 1950 the (estimated) Revenue was $31,610,791 and the (estimated) Expenditure $16,856,814. No grants-in-aid of administration were received during the year but the Colony received grants estimated at $2,185,573 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts.
CURRENCY AND BANKING
The amount of currency in circulation at the end of 1950 was estimated to be $34,008,000 of which $1,797,000 was pre-war Sarawak issue and $32,211,000 Malayan currency.
The Chartered Bank of India, Australia and China has a branch at Miri as well as at Kuching and Sibu.
PRODUCTION AND TRADE (See Note on page 27.)

During 1950 Sarawak's rural industries entered a period of unprecedented prosperity; rubber prices reached very high and sustained levels and the prices offered for pepper have also been high. Sago too has found a steady market.

Crude oil actually won in Sarawak during the year 1950 amounted to 56,601 tons. Next to petroleum, the chief imports are foodstuffs and manufactured goods, and the chief exports rubber, sago flour, pepper, timber, rattans, and jelutong.

Health

The Medical Department controls one well-equipped hospital at Victoria, and small, but adequately equipped, hospitals at Anse Royale, Praslin Island and La Digue Island. There is a mental hospital at Anse Royale and a settlement for the treatment of leprosy on Curieuse Island. Dispensaries at four other centres are held weekly.
There is also a quarantine station at Long Island and a home for the aged in Victoria.
A well-equipped bacteriological laboratory now exists adjacent to Victoria Hospital.
Intestinal disease forms the main health problem. There is no malaria, the anopheles mosquito being non-existent in the Seychelles.
The estimated expenditure on Medical and Health Services in 1950 was Rs. 421,865.

Income Tax is chargeable on all net income remaining after the following deductions unless the chargeable income is less than Rs. 500, in which case no tax is payable :—
Earned Income relief $\frac{1}{10}$th of earned income. (Maximum Rs.1,500) Resident's allowance Rs. 500 (or Rs. 2,000 for a married man living with or wholly maintaining his wife)
Child allowance .. Rs. 500 per child. (Maximum Rs.2,000) Dependent relative .. Rs. 500. Life insurance .. Amount of premiums. (Maximum $\frac{1}{6}$th of total income)

The rates of tax are on a sliding scale commencing at $2\frac{1}{2}$ per cent. on the first Rs. 3,000 of chargeable income and 5 per cent. on chargeable income between Rs. 3,000 and Rs. 6,000. Thereafter the rates of tax increase by $2\frac{1}{2}$ per cent. for each Rs. 1,000 of chargeable income up to a rate of $27\frac{1}{2}$ per cent. for chargeable income between Rs.14,000 and Rs. 15,000. For chargeable income between Rs.15,000 and Rs.25,000 the rate is 30 per cent. and between Rs. 25,000 and Rs. 40,000, 40 per cent. Over Rs. 40,000 the rate is 50 per cent.

CONSTITUTION

On 7th April, 1951, new Letters Patent issued, to take effect on a date to be notified by proclamation of the Governor, revoking the Letters Patent of 1924. These differ from the earlier Letters Patent principally in that they no longer provide for a Legislative Council and for legislative procedure. The Letters Patent took effect from the 27th November, 1951. New Royal Instructions dated 9th April, 1951, revoke the Instructions of 1924. These provide for an Executive Council of four ex officio members and not less than four unofficial members appointed from among the elected members of the Legislative Council. The Instructions also deal with legislative procedure. On 9th April, 1951, there issued new Orders of the King in Council, one to provide for the Protectorate, the other to provide for the Legislative Council, each revoking the corresponding Orders in Council of 1924. The former Instrument does not differ substantially from the Order in Council of 1924, but the latter provides for a new Legislative Council, to consist of 32 members including the Governor (who is President) and a Vice-President. There are seven ex officio members, seven members elected from the Colony districts, twelve members elected from the Protectorate District Councils, two members elected from the Protectorate Assembly, and two nominated

members appointed by the Governor. This Order in Council contains provisions in connection with legislative procedure which in 1924 were included in the Letters Patent. It does not, however, contain the procedure for the election of members to the Legislative Council, as did the Order in Council of 1924. This is now provided for in a local Statute, the Legislative Council (Elections) Ordinance, 1951.
ADMINISTRATION
Protectorate

In 1951, under the provisions of the District Councils Ordinance, 1950, the basis of representation in the District Councils was broadened by the addition of three members who may be either appointed by the Chiefdom representatives in the District Council or elected for a particular area. By the same Ordinance executive and financial responsibilities are vested in the Councils to enable them to take their full part in the economic development of the Protectorate. Their revenues are derived from Native Administrations, Government and from the Produce Marketing Board.

SINGAPORE AND DEPENDENCIES
See pages 356–370 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
HISTORY
The grant by Royal Charter of City Status to Singapore Municipality on the 22nd September, 1951 was a land-mark in the history of the Colony.
CONSTITUTION AND ADMINISTRATION Form of Government

The Executive Council over which the Governor presides consists of the 4 ex-officio members of the Legislative Council ( see below ), two other officials, and 6 unofficials including 2 elected by the unofficial members of the Legislative Council.

The Legislative Council now consists of the Governor as President (with an original and a casting vote) ; 4 ex-officio members (the Colonial and Financial Secretaries, the Attorney-General, and the President of the City Councillors) ; 5 nominated officials ; 3 elected severally by the Singapore, Chinese and Indian Chambers of Commerce ; 9 elected by universal suffrage of all British subjects and persons born in the British protected States in Malaya and Borneo over 21 on the annually-revised electoral register ; and 4 nominated unofficials. The Council thus has an unofficial majority. The nine elected members are returned from single-member constituencies. Those on the electoral register now number 46,622. The Council has an unofficial Vice-President who deputises for the President.
The City Council of Singapore consists of a President and 27 members.
Eighteen of the City Councillors are elected by popular vote, six elections being held each year. The City Electoral Register now includes 26,898 names.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

The enrolment in all registered schools is now 139,956 as compared with 124,113 a year ago. Of the number of pupils now in English schools, 16,773 receive remission of fees, while 17,025 pupils in other registered schools received assistance in paying school fees.
Expenditure in 1951 on education, excluding expenditure on public works, is estimated at $16,303,943. The actual figure in 1950 was $8,879,381.

The eighteen new schools in the 1950 Supplementary Education Plan were all completed, and are functioning, nine of them having both morning and afternoon sessions. One of the 1950 Supplementary schools has been completed and six others are under construction. Three schools scheduled under the Ten-Year Plan have been completed, one is under construction, and tenders for two others have been accepted. Extensions to two older English schools are under way. One new Malay school has been completed.

There are 1,480 teachers and students undergoing training at Government expense. 129 of these are enrolled in full-time courses at the Teachers' Training College, and 983 in part-time courses, the medium of instruction being English. There are twenty-one Singapore Malay students at the Sultan Idris Training College, and seven at the Malay Women's Training College. The remainder consist of teachers enrolled in training classes conducted in Chinese, Malay, and English. 1,305 students attend adult classes in technical subjects in the evenings.

The Government is now bearing the total expense of the teaching of English in Chinese schools. In addition, the grant-in-aid for all aided Chinese schools (containing sixty per cent of the total enrolment) has been doubled. A liaison officer has been appointed to make and preserve contact with Managing Committees, and schools are being encouraged to have their constitutions drawn up and submitted for approval.
Pressure on space in Malay schools continues to increase. The most important development has been a suggestion from the teachers, supported
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
by a large section of the parents, that the curriculum of these schools should be altered in such a way at to dove-tail into the "Regional" school scheme.

The Council for Adult Education, reference to which was made last year, is now firmly established. The Government voted it a sum of $173,000 for 1951; the Council has reached the target it set itself for 1951, and is proposing to double its work in 1952. There are four tutorial classes running, and in the literacy campaign there are 75 classes in English, 73 in Mandarin, 2 in Malay and 1 in Tamil. The Council is giving urgent attention to the provision of suitable headquarters. Its work, limited at present, is commended to every church, firm, trade union, social club and welfare organisation.

The Schools Broadcasts from Radio Malaya have further consolidated their position as an essential part of the educational system. The programmes are prepared and broadcast in Singapore but they serve the whole of the Federation in addition to the Colony. During 1951 the Malay and Chinese broadcasts particularly have shown development.
Department of Broadcasting
By the 31st August, 1951, 29,514 wireless licences had been issued in Singapore.
Broadcasts to schools now occupy 20½ hours per week, 9 in English, 7½ in Chinese, and 3½ in Malay.
Health
The Government Medical Department, in its 9 hospitals, now provide accommodation of up to 4,000 beds including the Mental Hospital.
There are now five static dispensaries and four travelling dispensaries for the rural population.
The Medical Plan which was approved in 1948, seeks to double the present services in all directions.

By the end of 1951, the new Medical Store and Manufactory will be completed and a further extension of accommodation will be provided at Trafalgar House (Hospital for Lepers). 1952 will see the beginning of a new Outpatients' Department and Nurses Hostel to accommodate 250 nurses at the General Hospital.
500 beds are provided at the T.B. clinic presented by the Rotary Club.

With the assistance of a World Health Organisation team of experts a B.C.G. campaign was successfully started in 1951 and this work is now being built up as a routine part of the medical department's work. If the average incidence of tuberculosis from 1939-1941 is taken as 100, the index which rose to 170 during the Japanese occupation fell to 52 in 1950. The problem remains urgent and plans for a sanatorium are now being made.
The Singapore Anti-Tuberculosis Association is also greatly extending its accommodation.

Hospital out-patient attendances which were under 100,000 in pre-war years rose to 433,000 in 1950. Tuberculosis out-patients increased from a negligible number before the war to 43,000 in 1950. In the rural and maternity services, clinics have increased in number from 11 before the war to 39 in 1950: attendances have increased in the same period from 85,000 to 170,000. Homes visited by nurses now total 28,000 and by midwives 47,000. In the villages, midwives are
attending 4-5 times as many deliveries as are considered the maximum in the United Kingdom. Two new Infant Welfare Clinics are under construction.
The key problem remains one of staff and accommodation. Plans are, however, on foot for intensive recruiting of nurses after the provision of the new Nurses Hostel has been completed.

Although the population is increasing by some 30,000 a year the number of deaths dropped from 14,200 in 1939 to 12,300 in 1950 and infantile deaths from 4,500 to 3,800. The infant mortality rate has fallen from 130.47 in 1939, and about 300 during the occupation, to 82.2 in 1950.
Labour

The rise in the cost of living, which occurred shortly after the rise in rubber and tin prices to high levels early in 1951, brought a crop of negotiated agreements between workers and employers giving increases in wage rates or cost of living allowances. According to records kept by the Labour Department, there were negotiated increases in 23 different industries, and there is little doubt that there was a general rise in rates over the whole field of employment.

The declaration of the Emergency in June, 1948, led to the disappearance of many of the leaders of the communist-dominated trade unions. During the three years which have elapsed since then, the trade union movement has made useful progress, the idea of voluntary negotiation having gained much ground. There were three strikes in the early part of 1951, two of them lasting over three weeks each. While the right to strike has been in no way restricted as a result of the Emergency, under the Emergency (Strikes and Lock-outs) Regulations 1948, fourteen days notice of strike or lock-out must be given by workers or employers. A Trade Union Congress was formed in 1951. The total membership of Unions is about 53,000.
A tripartite delegation from Singapore attended the Asian Regional Conference of the I.L.O. in Ceylon in January, 1950.

The 1947 amendment to the Workmen's Compensation Ordinance bringing labourers earning up to $400 a month within its scope was extended into 1951. A new Workmen's Compensation Ordinance is under preparation.
Welfare

The Department of Social Welfare carries out statutory and administrative functions in the social field which are not provided by existing Government Departments. The Colony's numerous and well organised voluntary agencies, with whom the Department works in close partnership, cover an important segment of the welfare field. The Department operates such residual institutions or services as are not, or cannot conveniently be, established and maintained by voluntary effort alone.

The Department acts as a co-ordinating agency between the various voluntary bodies as well as those Departments of Government whose activities impinge upon the field of social welfare. The medium by which this is carried out is the Social Welfare Council, consisting of representatives of the most prominent voluntary bodies including the churches, as well as heads of the Departments of Education, Medicine and Labour,
Digitized by Google
SINGAPORE
and representatives of the City Council and the Rural Board. Government grants to voluntary bodies are administered by the Department.

The Department is the body which exercises statutory functions under the Women and Girls' Ordinance and the Children's Ordinance. In this it co-operates with the Police, the Immigration Department, and the Medical Department, carrying out raids on notorious premises, screening all female immigrants, conducting prosecutions and maintaining a large number of women and children in need of care and protection in three institutions as well as inspecting similar institutions run by voluntary bodies.

Indoor and outdoor assistance is financed partly from revenue and partly by trust funds and voluntary organisations. The Tuberculosis Treatment Allowances Scheme, introduced in 1949, is a primary weapon in the Colony's action against tuberculosis. Its aim is to rehabilitate tuberculosis victims in the early stages whilst cure is still possible. A new scheme for public assistance was brought into force on the 1st August, 1951, and this scheme as well as the Tuberculosis Treatment Allowances Scheme are under constant review and revision as experience is gained in their operation.

The Probationary Service has now been extended to the Adult Courts. Hitherto, the Probationary Service had worked only in conjunction with the Juvenile Court, which sits in the Social Welfare Department building. The Department is responsible for the management of institutions for the detention of juveniles, and is responsible for the administration of the legislation providing for the registration of transferred children. An advice and enquiry service is in existence helping individuals in such matters as making claims, settling disputes and tracing relatives.

The Department runs 18 Children's Social Centres, which are attended by some of the poorest children between the ages of 2 and 14. In the early days of these Centres, the emphasis lay largely on the feeding aspect, but more and more emphasis is now being put on the social and educational potentialities which the Centres have been shown to possess in over-crowded areas, where schooling, club and community facilities are not yet available to the whole population. These Centres owe their success largely to a body of voluntary workers, representative of all communities in Singapore.

The Department seeks to stimulate the establishment of boys' and girls' clubs and hostels and where possible to run such institutions through the agency of voluntary bodies, the Department providing funds and advice when required. In this work the Department collaborates with the Singapore Youth Council and the Federation of Boys' Clubs, both independent organisations.

The training of officers of the Department, both in Singapore and in the United Kingdom, has proceeded satisfactorily. Two senior officers of the Department, who have been studying in London for the last three years, are expected back before the end of 1951. A further two senior officers will proceed to the United Kingdom for specialised training during the course of 1952. Slowly, but surely, the Department is building up a body of professional officers specially trained in various aspects of Social Welfare work.
Through the Social Research Section of the Department, it is hoped to plan surveys which will be of great value in the future social and economic planning of Singapore.
COMMUNICATIONS
Shipping and Port Facilities
The total length of the wharves of the Singapore Harbour Board is now 13,000 feet. The area of storage warehouses has increased to 800,000 sq. feet.
The total capacity of the dockyard power station is now 6,500 kilowatts.

For the year ended 30th June, 1951, 2,873 vessels of a total registered tonnage of 8,144,343 tons were berthed at the wharves. This is the highest figure since the war. The amount of cargo dealt with in the same period was 2,608,915 tons inward and 2,338,357 outward. These figures include fuel, oil and coal. During the same period, 416 vessels were docked for repairs and painting.

SOMALILAND PROTECTORATE
See pages 371–374 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
POPULATION
No complete census has been taken but the population of the Protectorate is now estimated at from 500,000 to 700,000.
ADMINISTRATION
The Protectorate Advisory Council continues to be an important instrument in the Government of the country. Good progress has been made in the setting up of Local Authorities and District Councils.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Health

It is intended to give short training in simple first-aid and home-nursing to persons nominated by the tribes. These tribal dressers will be supplied with limited quantities of drugs and dressings but will not be paid members of the Medical Department.
COMMUNICATIONS
A radio telephone now operates between Hargeisa and the district headquarters.
PUBLIC REVENUE AND EXPENDITURE (See Note on page 27.)
The Revenue of the Protectorate for the financial year 1950–51 amounted to £494,096 and the Expenditure to £1,093,797.
CURRENCY AND BANKING

Indian currency was legal tender for many years. East African currency was introduced shortly after the return of British forces to the Protectorate in 1941 and became legal tender concurrently with Indian currency early in 1942. The post-war years saw a gradual extension in the use of East African currency due to government policy of withdrawing rupees from circulation. Finally a general withdrawal and exchange was organized in October, 1951 (to coincide with similar operations in Aden), and Indian currency ceased to be legal tender on 31st October, 1951.

By the time these notes are published a branch bank will have been opened in the capital, Hargeisa, and the normal banking business of the Protectorate (including the Government account), hitherto conducted in Aden, will doubtless have been transferred to it.
There is also a Government Savings Bank in the Protectorate.
TRADE
(See Note on page 27.)

Imports in 1950 were valued at £1,217,448 and Exports at £726,040. The main imports were rice, dates and other fruit, tea, sugar, yarn and textile fabrics and petrol. The main exports were sheep and sheep and goat skins.

SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
European. The total enrolment in schools for the education of European children in early 1951 was 1,328 pupils.
Three English privately maintained schools at Kongwa, Urambo and Nachingwea were taken over by Government in 1951, the former being a boarding school and supplying secondary education.
Indian. There were 76 schools with a total enrolment of 12,552. Grants-in-aid are paid to 70 schools in respect of staff, buildings and

There were two Goan schools with an enrolment of 626, including 176 Indians.
African. 381 schools were maintained by Government or the Native Authorities, the total enrolment in the primary schools being 46,625.
Assistance was given to 933 schools run by voluntary agencies whose total enrolment was 125,080. The number of secondary and Middle schools of this group increased to 29.

Staff. The African establishment of the Education Department in 1951 included 1,502 teachers and 72 industrial instructors.
Health
The largest hospital group is at Dar-es-Salaam with accommodation for a total of 487 of all races.

Government leprosaria are maintained at Chazi in the Eastern Province and Makete in the Southern Highlands. In addition a number of Government subsidised leprosaria are maintained by the Missions in various parts of the Territory.
Specialist services include medicine, surgery, pathology, tuberculosis, ophthalmology and sleeping sickness.
The headquarters of the inter-territorial anti-malarial unit is now at Amani in the Northern Province.
A Departmental Nutrition Officer is at present working with the East African Medical Survey at Mwanza.
Labour
Three Regional Assistant Labour Commissioners and a Trade Testing Inspector have been added to the Labour Department Staff.

On the lines of communication, Government have established various camps and shelters for the free use of African labourers travelling to and from the employment areas which are maintained by this Department; so also have the professional and private recruiters, the latter including organisations of employers. At these camps food is provided and a dresser and first-aid facilities are also available.

Twenty Labour Exchanges are now in operation in the Territory with the object of placing skilled and semi-skilled workers in touch with potential employers. These exchanges are linked together at the headquarters where a further subsidiary exchange deals with applications for employment from districts where no labour exchange exists. Labour exchanges are established at the following more important employment areas and district headquarters :—
Arusha ; Bukoba ; Dar-es-Salaam ; Dodoma ; Iringa ; Kilosa ; Korogwe ; Lindi ; Mbeya ; Morogoro ; Moshi ; Musoma ; Mwanza ; Njombe ; Same ; Shinyanga ; Songea ; Tabora ; Tanga ; Tukuyu.

TRINIDAD
POPULATION.
The population of Trinidad and Tobago on 31st December, 1950, was estimated to be 635,843.
CONSTITUTION AND ADMINISTRATION

Unofficial members of the Executive Council may be charged with the administration of particular Departments and subjects. In the present Executive Council all five elected members have been given Ministerial responsibilities associating them with the work of Government Departments.

Delete the sentence “Two councillors are elected for each ward . . . . . district” in the sixth paragraph under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 and now read :—“Two councillors are elected for each ward and each County Council appoints a councillor as Chairman”.
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
The number of pupils on the roll of the primary and intermediate schools in December, 1950, was 115,309. As compared with the previous year there was an increase of 4,513 on the roll.
The total expenditure on education during the year 1950, deducting the amount received in school and examination fees, was $5,226,609 as compared with $4,781,496 in 1949.

To the list of colleges which afford facilities for the higher education of boys given under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 , should be added :—The College of St. Philip and St. James in Chaguanas.
Information Services
The newsletter published by the Information Office is now issued fortnightly.
Health
The expenditure by the Health Department in 1950 was $4,984,206, representing 9.9% of the total expenditure of the Colony.

There are now on the island three Colonial Hospitals, a Colony Mental Hospital, a Leprosarium at Chacachacare, a Tuberculosis Hospital, a Tuberculosis Sanatorium, a Venereal Diseases Hospital, two Houses of Refuge, and eight District Hospitals.
Health Centres and rural dispensaries are maintained in townships and larger villages.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads
The City of Port of Spain, apart from suburban areas, is now served exclusively by trolley buses.
Railways

The total number of passengers carried during 1950 was 9,240,486, and the amount of goods transported was 479,858 tons. The total receipts from the railway, telegraph, and road services in 1950 amounted to $1,930,648.
Shipping
To the list of steamship lines calling at Trinidad given under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 , the New Zealand Shipping Company should be added.
In 1950, the total net tonnage of shipping entered and cleared was 9,943,165, of which British net tonnage totalled 3,166,048.
Air

Regular passenger air mail and cargo services are maintained and operated by British West Indian Airways Ltd., Pan American Airways Inc., K.L.M. (Royal Dutch Airlines), Linea Aeropostal Venezolana, Trans-Canada Air Lines, Aerovias Brasil, British Guiana Airways, Aero-lineas Argentinas, Avianca Airlines and Iberia Airlines. The last three operate on a “non-traffic” basis. Through the medium of these services the Colony is directly linked up with other British West Indian Islands, the Dutch and French West Indies, the Virgin Islands, British, French and Dutch Guiana, British Honduras, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Haiti, Puerto Rico, Argentina, Uruguay, Brazil, Venezuela, Colombia, Panama, Bermuda, United States and Canada; and by connecting services with the United Kingdom and other parts of the world. During 1950 there were 13,709 aircraft movements. The total number of passengers carried was 113,328 and the amount of mail and freight handled was 356,518 lbs. and 1,611,275 lbs. respectively.
PUBLIC FINANCE
(See Note on page 27.)

In 1950 the Revenue totalled $50,619,150; the Expenditure $50,430,561. The Public Debt at 31st December 1950 stood at $42,124,086. Grants received from His Majesty's Government in the United Kingdom under Colonial Development and Welfare Acts during the year amounted to $389,679. No grants in aid of Administration were received.
The main sources of revenue in 1950 were customs and excise, $17,636,815; tax on incomes, $17,540,406; licences and other inland revenue, $3,621,785; forests, lands and petroleum, $6,217,656.

The main headings of expenditure in 1950 were public debt, $2,114,562; social services, including health and education, $10,223,484; works and hydraulics, extraordinary and annually recurrent, $8,941,734; railways and telegraphs, including road transport, $3,049,717; police, $3,018,666; defence, $41,827.
Some changes have been made in allowances which may be deducted in assessing income tax liabilities.

PUBLIC DEBT

The Public Debt of $42,124,086 at 31st December, 1950 was made up of $18,933,686 local loans and $23,190,400 external loans. Of the total, $3,825,728 was raised on behalf of the Imperial Treasury, and $13,050,206 for other authorities.
CURRENCY AND BANKING
The total value of Government currency notes in circulation at 31st December, 1950, was $21,369,249.

The currency comprises dollar notes (1 dollar = 50 pence) and British silver and bronze coin. Trinidad unified its currency note system with that of the other British territories in the Eastern Caribbean (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward and the Windward Islands) in 1951, and the currency now includes notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board (Trinidad Government notes are being withdrawn). Legislative provision also exists for the eventual unification of coin in the British Eastern Caribbean territories.
The total number of depositors with Government savings banks at the 31st December, 1950, was 103,681, with deposits amounting to $10,572,376.

Asphalt. The quantity of asphalt produced in 1950 was 132,846 tons, compared with 145,160 tons in 1949.
Petroleum. Production of crude oil in 1950 amounted to 20,632,421 barrels, compared with 20,616,721 barrels in 1949.

Sugar. The 1950 sugar crop yielded 146,508 tons, compared with 158,890 tons in 1949. Peasant farmers produced 36.81 per cent of a total of 1,401,931 tons of cane milled. At the end of the year there were six sugar factories.
The export of molasses in 1950 was 409,358 cwt. The greater part of the remainder of the supply was used in the manufacture of rum.

Coconuts. Copra milled in 1950 was 35,408,000 lb. compared with 35,353,920 lb. in 1949. With the exception of 1,568,000 lb., which was exported, all the copra produced in 1950 was delivered to the factories for oil extraction and processing.

Citrus. There are now about 14,500 acres under citrus. Good crops of oranges and grapefruit were obtained but the lime crop was indifferent. Exports of all citrus and citrus products during 1950 were valued at $2,176,231 as compared with $1,163,969 in 1949.
Coffee. The acreage of this crop has increased owing largely to replacements under the Cocoa Subsidy Scheme. Heavy rains had a depressing

effect on yields, particularly that of the Arabica crop. Returns from the Robusta crop were more satisfactory, but less than those obtained in 1949. In 1950 a total of 1,636,199 lb. of raw coffee and 57,101 lb. of prepared coffee was exported, compared with 2,344,817 lb. of raw coffee and 52,369 lb. of prepared coffee in 1949.
Other Crops. Exports of tonca beans during 1950 totalled 189,639 lb. valued at $224,973 compared with 195,079 lb. valued at $144,612 in 1949.
The output of rubber increased during the year and the export of raw rubber was 20,124 lb. valued at $16,535 in comparison with 2,458 lb. valued at $590 in 1949.
Exports of fresh vegetables during the year totalled 497,392 lb. valued at $76,450 as compared with 470,731 lb. valued at $86,526 in 1949.

European primary schools in Uganda contained 232 pupils and a further 304 European children attended primary and secondary schools in Kenya.
1,896 students were undergoing training at the 41 Primary and Vernacular Teacher Training Centres.

In the field of higher education, Makerere College, which is situated in Uganda, is an autonomous institution financed by the East African Governments, with assistance under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts. In 1950 this College was admitted to special relations with the University of London and was recognised as the University College of East Africa. In that year the College had 230 students including seven women; 82 men and five women were from Uganda.
Four Government scholars returned from study overseas during 1950, eight scholars began their studies overseas and some 12 more were chosen for the 1951 session.
PUBLIC FINANCE (See Note on page 27.)

The main sources of revenue are Export Taxes (principally on cotton and coffee), Customs and Excise, Income Tax and Native and Non-Native Poll Tax. The revised 1950 revenue estimates for Export Taxes were £4,040,755, and for Customs and Excise, £2,389,000. The corresponding figures for 1951 are £7,593,500 and £3,478,000. The Public Debt consists of £850,000 of Uganda Government 3½ per cent. inscribed stock, 1955–1965, raised under Ordinances Nos. 1 of 1932 and 24 of 1937, and £3,100,000 of Uganda Government 3½ per cent. inscribed stock, 1966–1969, raised under Ordinance No. 8 of 1950. The unit of currency is the East African Shilling.

The figures for Revenue and Expenditure for 1949 in the table on page 402 of The Colonial Office List 1951 should be replaced by :—" Revenue :— £8,094,381 " and " Expenditure. —£6,686,863 " The figures for 1950 are £9,905,164 and £7,999,440 respectively and for 1951, £14,733,314 and £12,804,803 respectively.

SOCIAL SERVICES
Education

The various missionary societies continue to play a leading part in the educational system of the Protectorate. In 1950 there were 1,352 Government or grant-aided African primary schools and of these 1,295 were Mission-owned. The total number of children attending these schools was 158,900, of whom 37,800 were girls. It is not possible to obtain accurate figures for Mission or Muslim schools not aided by Government, but it is estimated that these may contain up to the same number of pupils as those in the aided system. There were in 1950 50 Government or aided African secondary schools, of which 41 were Mission-owned. These schools contained 5,056 pupils, of whom 645 were girls.
The education of the Asian community was provided for by eight Government and 70 aided schools, of which 74 were primary and four
CURRENCY AND BANKING

To the list of branches of the National Bank of India, Ltd., given under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 should be added Masaka, Jinja, Soroti and Fort Portal; to the list of branches of the Standard Bank of South Africa, Ltd., should be added Masaka and Soroti; and to those of Barclays Bank (D.C. & O.), Tororo and Fort Portal.
PRODUCTION AND TRADE

The principal economic crops of Uganda are cotton and coffee. Cotton, which is cultivated almost entirely by Africans, is grown as a rain crop without the assistance of irrigation, with the result that there is a somewhat wide variation from year to year in the total crop produced. But the general level of production shows a steady upward trend. In the 1949/50 season 1,628,000 acres were planted. This was the second highest acreage ever attained in the Protectorate and yielded a crop amounting to 342,000 bales.

UGANDA

Both Robusta and Arabica varieties of coffee are grown, production in 1949/50 exceeding 32,000 tons. Other economic crops are groundnuts, tobacco and tea. Salted fish and crocodile skins are important items of export. Recent investigations have shown that Uganda contains valuable mineral deposits including gold, copper, cobalt, wolfram, magnetite, apatite, niobium and zirconium.

PRODUCTION AND TRADE
(See Note on page 27.)

In 1950 the total imports of the Condominium were valued at £527,983, of which goods to the value of £334,035 were imported from Australia, £107,244 from France, £15,462 from the U.S.A., £7,375 from the U.K., the balance valued at £63,867 being imported from other countries. The exports for the year were valued at £1,425,003 of which goods to the value of £1,232,348 were exported to France, £58,803 to Australia, Nil to the U.S.A., Nil to the U.K., and £133,852 to other countries.

During 1946 and 1947, owing to restrictions of market and shipping difficulties elsewhere, import trade veered sharply towards the U.S.A., but from 1948 onwards, owing to dollar shortage and improvement in export conditions relating to other markets, trade with the U.S.A. was reduced to under 3% of imports in 1950 and increased with other sources, principally Australia 63%, France 20% and Hong Kong 6% of imports.

The greater part of the territory's exports (86.5% by value in 1950) including all copra, now goes to France; a small proportion, however (4.1% in 1950 comprising some cacao and all timber) goes to Australia. The largest export is copra (21,569 metric tons valued at £1,265,372 Stg. and yielding £31,554 in export duty in 1950). Since the war the f.o.b. price of copra has fluctuated as follows:—

WINDWARD ISLANDS
THE WINDWARD ISLANDS
See pages 419–434 of The Colonial Office List 1951.
CONSTITUTION

Each of the four colonies of Grenada, St. Vincent, St. Lucia and Dominica retains its own institutions. The Governor is normally resident in Grenada where he is assisted by a Chief Secretary of the Windward Islands. The government of each colony is administered by a resident Administrator subject to instructions from the Governor. There is no common Legislature, nor common laws, revenue or tariff. Each colony has a Legislative Council presided over by the Administrator, in which the majority of members are elected by universal adult suffrage; and each colony has an Executive Council which may be summoned and consulted by either the Governor or the Administrator. A Court of Appeal for the West Indies consisting of the chief justices of the several West Indies Islands except Jamaica was set up in 1920 by Imperial Act (9 and 10 Geo. V. c. 47) and a Supreme Court and Court of Appeal for the Windward Islands and Leeward Islands were established by the Leeward Islands and Windward Islands (Courts) Order-in-Council 1939. There is also a common audit system, instituted in 1889.
COMMUNICATIONS
The temporary service with Grumman Goose amphibian aircraft is now operated by British Guiana Airways between St. Lucia, St. Vincent and Dominica (and not between Grenada, St. Vincent and Dominica).
REVENUE AND EXPENDITURE OF THE WINDWARD ISLANDS
(See Note on page 27.)
In 1949 the combined Revenue amounted to $8,692,588 and the combined Expenditure to $9,349,489. The provisional figures for 1950 are $10,398,816 and $10,141,578 respectively.
CURRENCY AND BANKING

The currency comprises dollar notes ( $1 = 4/2d. ) and British coinage. Notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board were introduced in 1951 when the Windward Islands joined with the other British territories in the Eastern Caribbean (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands and Trinidad) in currency unification. Trinidad Government notes continue as legal tender but are being withdrawn and the Banks' powers of note issue have been terminated. Legislative provision has been made for eventual unification of coin in the Eastern Caribbean including the Windwards.
Barclays Bank (Dominion, Colonial and Overseas) Ltd. and the Royal Bank of Canada have branches in the larger islands.

By an Order in Council dated 1st August, 1951, which came into operation on 29th September, 1951, the Legislative Council was re-constituted; it now consists of the Administrator as President, two other ex-officio members (the Crown Attorney and Financial Secretary) three nominated members (being persons not holding a public office in the Colony) and eight elected members (one for each of eight electoral districts) elected by universal adult suffrage. Property and income qualifications of candidates for elected membership were abolished. The Governor may address the Council at any time he thinks fit. No change was made in the Governor's reserve powers. At the same time the Executive Council was reconstituted by Royal Instructions; it now consists of the Administrator, if the Governor is present in the Executive Council, and two other ex-officio members (the persons who are ex-officio members of the Legislative Council), one official member appointed by the Governor, who must be a person holding a public office in the Colony, one nominated member appointed by the Governor from among the nominated members of the Legislative Council, and three elected members who are elected by the Legislative Council from among the elected members of that Council.

WINDWARD ISLANDS
SOCIAL SERVICES
Education
The annual value of the agricultural scholarship tenable at the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture is now £225.
A scholarship in Veterinary Surgery has also been awarded.
Public Relations
The monthly paper issued by the Public Relations Office and previously named "The St. Vincent Information Service" is now named "Challenge".
Water Supply
The rural extensions of the distribution system are being installed and will bring pipe-borne water to about 20,000 people in the southern area of the island.

The Hydro-Electric Scheme, work on which was commenced in 1948 but later discontinued, has been taken over by the Colonial Development Corporation, and work is to be started on the construction of a hydro-electric plant on the Colonarie River which is estimated to cost £200,000.

Intake works and a power-house are to be constructed on the Colonarie River with power distribution to Kingstown (the capital), Mesopotamia, Georgetown and Camden Park. Power will also be available for the principal factories in the island which process arrowroot and sea island cotton, as well as for use by the ice-making and cold-storage plant.
COMMUNICATIONS
Roads

A road construction programme for the period 1950-56 is in progress and at the end of the period it is estimated that 25 more miles of road will have been asphalted and four new bridges built at a cost of $370,000.
Airfields
The small airfield situated at Diamond is no longer in use.
With regard to the alighting area for small flying boats at Villa Bay, a ramp and hangar have been built on the Villa shore in order that amphibian aircraft can proceed from the water to the land.

THE HIGH COMMISSION TERRITORIES
(BASUTOLAND, THE BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE AND SWAZILAND)

The Territories of Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland in southern Africa are administered by the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations through a High Commissioner appointed by the Queen. The High Commissioner also holds the office of High Commissioner for the United Kingdom in the Union of South Africa. He is the sole legislative authority for these Territories and is responsible to the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations in much the same way as a Colonial Governor is responsible to the Secretary of State for the Colonies.
Each Territory is administered by a Resident Commissioner, responsible to the High Commissioner, and is divided into administrative districts in charge of District Commissioners.

Information about these Territories will be found in The Commonwealth Relations Office List . This brief note, and the following Civil Establishment list showing heads of departments and analogous appointments, have been included in The Colonial Office List 1952 because the Territories' Services are interchangeable with the Colonial Service. When full editions of The Colonial Office List are resumed it is intended to include, in the Record of Services section, biographical notes of appropriate officers serving in the Territories.

REGIONAL ORGANISATIONS
REGIONAL ORGANISATIONS

A feature of British colonial policy is the encouragement of regional co-operation between Colonies, wherever practicable. In this way there is not only a valuable sharing of knowledge and experience, but also, in certain instances, a substantial measure of economy from the unified control of technical services. The following are the principal regional organisations.
CENTRAL AFRICAN COUNCIL
The following passage replaces that appearing under the heading Rhodesia-Nyasaland Inter-territorial Conference on page 456 of The Colonial Office List, 1951 .

The Central African Council was set up in 1945, its objects being to promote the closest possible contact and co-ordination of policy and action between the Governments of Southern Rhodesia, Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland on all matters of common interest to the three territories. Eleven meetings have been held.

The Governor of Southern Rhodesia is Chairman of the Council which includes the Governors of Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland and the Prime Minister of Southern Rhodesia and nine other members. The Council has a permanent Secretariat under a Chief Secretary.

Joint services have been established to deal with Town Planning, Statistics, Archives, Meteorology and the production of films. There are Inter-territorial Standing Committees on Public Relations, Broadcasting, Health, Trypanosomiasis, African Labour and Research.

In the latter part of 1950 the establishment of a standing advisory Rhodesia-Nyasaland Inter-territorial Conference to replace the Central African Council was agreed to by the Secretary of State for the Colonies and by the Governments of Southern Rhodesia, Northern Rhodesia and Nyasaland. However, in consequence of a re-examination of the closer political association of the three territories and pending a decision by the Governments regarding the recommendations of the Closer Association Conference held in London in March, 1951, no alteration to the existing inter-territorial machinery is to be made.

At the meeting of the East Africa High Commission held in March, 1951, proposals were approved for the creation of an Air Advisory Council, with the Commissioner for Transport as ex-officio Chairman, two members appointed by each of the Governments of Kenya, Tanganyika and Uganda and one by the British Resident, Zanzibar, and, in addition, two members appointed by the High Commission.
The Air Advisory Council has now been set up by administrative action pending the necessary legal enactment to establish the Council as a statutory body. The functions of the Council are :—
To be advisory to the East Africa High Commission, whose authority is exercised through the Commissioner for Transport ;

To advise on all questions of major policy affecting Civil Aviation within the jurisdiction of the East Africa High Commission and on any civil aviation matters referred to it by the East Africa High Commission acting through the Commissioner for Transport ;
To advise on the Annual Estimates of the Directorate of Civil Aviation before they are submitted to the Central Legislative Assembly and the Estimates Committee of the Central Legislative Assembly ;
To advise on all Civil Aviation legislation coming with the scope of the Central Legislative Assembly ;
To advise on the steps necessary to achieve a policy of co-ordination between Civil Aviation and all other forms of transport.

RESEARCH AND SCIENTIFIC SERVICES
Research Services
The six principal research divisions are:—

The East African Agricultural and Forestry Research Organisation which is responsible inter alia for studies on soils, fertiliser investigations in all Territories, plant pathology and ecology, entomology especially termites, silviculture, and clove research in Zanzibar. New headquarters, shared with the Veterinary Research Organisation (and with which, jointly, a Division of Animal Industry is maintained), have been established at Muguga within easy reach of Nairobi.

The East African Veterinary Research Organisation. This organisation is responsible for research on animal diseases, animal husbandry and other aspects of the animal industry. At present its headquarters are at Kabete in Kenya but will be moved to Muguga when new buildings are completed.

The East African Tsetse and Trypanosomiasis Research and Reclamation Organisation. This Organisation has central offices in Nairobi and laboratories for tsetse research at Old Shinyanga in Tanganyika. A new central institute for trypanosomiasis research is to be established at Sukulu in Uganda and there are sub-stations for experimental reclamation of land from tsetse fly in the three territories.

The East African Fisheries Research Organisation, which is in two sections, one for the fisheries of inland waters with headquarters at Jinja in Uganda, and the other for marine fisheries at Zanzibar.

The East African Industrial Research Board, which has headquarters in Nairobi. It is at present concerned with research on secondary industries but plans exist for establishing an East African Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation which will incorporate the Industrial Research Board and will have wider functions.

The Medical Research Division, which includes the East African Bureau of Research in Medicine and Hygiene with an office in Nairobi, the Virus Research Institute at Entebbe in Uganda, the Medical Survey and Filariasis Research unit based on Mwanza in Tanganyika, the Malaria unit at Muheza in Tanganyika and a small Leprosy Unit based on Nairobi.
Scientific Services
The headquarters of the Lake Victoria Fisheries Service are now at Kisumu in Kenya.
A large organisation for locust control has been established temporarily to meet the threat of new invasions.

WEST AFRICAN INTER-TERRITORIAL CONFERENCE
See page 456 of The Colonial Office List 1951 under the heading West African Council .

During 1950 and early 1951 a review of the machinery for inter-territorial consultation in West Africa was carried out, particular regard being paid to the recent and impending constitutional changes in West Africa which made it desirable that Africans should take a greater

part in inter-territorial consultation and collaboration. As a result the Secretary of State (Mr. James Griffiths) announced on the 30th May, 1951, the dissolution of the West African Council and the setting up in its place of the West African Inter-Territorial Conference. The Conference, which will meet at least once a year and which will hold its first meeting in 1952, consists of two members of the Executive Council (or Council of Ministers) from each British West African territory, nominated by the Governor-in-Council. The Chairman of the Conference is the Governor of Nigeria or, in his absence, a deputy nominated by him after consultation with the other Governors. The functions of the Conference are as follows :
(a) to receive from the Secretariat a report on inter-territorial collaboration on research matters and to review the progress made thereon ;

(b) to receive from the Secretariat a report on inter-territorial collaboration in other social and economic matters as expressed in reports of conferences held during the previous year or in other convenient form and to make recommendations thereon ;
(c) to consider international collaboration on matters covered by (a) and (b) and to make recommendations thereon ;
(d) to consider the estimates for the West African Inter-territorial Secretariat ;
(e) to consider any other matters referred to it by any West African Government.

The Conference has taken over the permanent Secretariat of the West African Council under the title of the West African Inter-Territorial Secretariat. The Secretariat continues to be charged broadly with the duties performed by the Secretariat of the West African Council, including in particular those of fostering international collaboration with non-British territories in West Africa, organizing interterritorial collaboration on technical subjects, supervising the administration of joint research services and co-ordinating the military and civil aspects of West African defence. The Headquarters of the Secretariat remain at Accra.
The costs of the organization are borne by the four West African Governments.
Chief Secretary—A. N. Galsworthy. £1,500 × 75—£1,800 × 100—£2,000 plus a consolidated allowance of £1,200 per annum payable only in West Africa.

The scheme is a three-year programme designed to provide participating countries with technical assistance in the form of training facilities and the services of experts. PART III THE COLONIAL SERVICE RECRUITMENT

The term "The Colonial Service" is a wide one. It embraces all the public services of all those British Colonies* for the administration of which the Secretary of State for the Colonies is responsible. These are :—
(a) AFRICA
West Africa : Nigeria, the Gold Coast, Sierra Leone and the Gambia.
East Africa : Kenya, Uganda, Tanganyika, Somaliland Protectorate and Zanzibar.
Central Africa : Northern Rhodesia and the Nyasaland Protectorate.
(b) SOUTH-EAST ASIA AND FAR EAST
The Federation of Malaya, Singapore, Sarawak, Brunei, North Borneo and Hong Kong.
(c) WEST INDIES
Jamaica, Trinidad, Barbados, British Guiana, British Honduras, Leeward Islands and Windward Islands.
(d) WESTERN PACIFIC
Fiji, Gilbert and Ellice Islands, British Solomon Islands Protectorate.
(e) MEDITERRANEAN
Cyprus, Gibraltar and Malta.
(f) MIDDLE EAST Aden.
(g) Bahama Islands, Bermuda, Falkland Islands, Mauritius, St. Helena and Seychelles.

The Government of each of these Territories is a separate administrative entity. Members of the Colonial Service serving in any Colony are public servants of that Colony and their salaries are paid from local revenue. The Colonial Service thus differs from other British public services in that

it is not a single service for purposes of administration and discipline. Opportunities occur, however, for members of each Colony's public service to be considered for promotion or transfer to the public service of other Colonies.

It is the declared policy of Her Majesty's Government to encourage the progress of the peoples of the Colonies towards control of their own affairs. If this policy is to be a reality, the public services of the Colonies must be adapted to local conditions and be staffed to the greatest possible extent by local people. Overseas staff therefore are in general recruited only for those posts in the Colonial Service for which sufficient suitable and qualified local candidates are not available. Since for one reason or another many Colonies will be unable for a long time to come to find from their own resources enough suitable and qualified staff to meet all the needs of modern administration, there remains and will remain a wide range of appointments open to candidates from overseas.

There are four separate recruiting organisations in the United Kingdom dealing with appointments for candidates from outside the Colonies : the Director of Recruitment (Colonial Service), Colonial Office, Sanctuary Buildings, Great Smith Street, London, S.W.1., for appointments for which the Secretary of State for the Colonies selects candidates, on the advice of the Colonial Service Appointments Board ; the Under-Secretary of State, Research Department, Colonial Office, Sanctuary Buildings, Great Smith Street, London, S.W.1, for appointments in Her Majesty's Colonial Research Service ; the Overseas Nursing Association, 15, Victoria Street, London S.W.1, for appointments in Queen Elizabeth's Colonial Nursing Service, for which the Secretary of State for the Colonies selects candidates on the recommendation of the Committee of the Association ; the Crown Agents for the Colonies, 4, Millbank, London, S.W.1, for appointments for which they, acting on behalf of Colonial Governments, select and engage candidates.

All these appointments are described in the pamphlets Appointments in His Majesty's Colonial Service (H.M. Stationery Office.) and Appointments in His Majesty's Colonial Research Service (obtainable from the Under Secretary of State, Research Department, Colonial Office).
*The term "Colonies" is used, for convenience, to cover colonies in the strict sense of the term, protectorates, protected states and trust territories.
